proposals.title: Programmable Peptide-Peptide Nucleic Acid (PNA) Hybrids as Robust, Non-DNA Bonding Elements for Colloidal Crystal Engineering
proposals.motivation: DNA-programmable colloidal crystallization (DPCC), pioneered by the Mirkin group, has revolutionized materials assembly but is fundamentally limited by the chemical and biological instability of DNA oligonucleotides in non-aqueous, high-temperature, or biologically active environments. To access new classes of functional materials, particularly for nanophotonics and sensing, there is a critical need for programmable bonding elements with enhanced stability and orthogonal chemical functionality. This proposal addresses a key unresolved question in nanoscience: how to achieve DNA-like programmability with non-nucleic acid linkers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that short, rationally designed peptide sequences, conjugated to synthetic peptide nucleic acid (PNA) recognition strands, can serve as programmable, thermally robust, and chemically versatile 'bonding elements' to direct the assembly of nanoparticles into crystalline superlattices with predictable structure and emergent optical properties.
proposals.method: We will design a library of complementary PNA-peptide chimeras, where the PNA segment provides sequence-specific hybridization (replacing DNA-DNA recognition) and the peptide segment dictates interparticle spacing and interaction valency via its length and terminal functional groups (e.g., biotin-streptavidin, His-tag-Ni-NTA). Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and halide perovskite nanocrystals will be functionalized with these chimeras. Assembly will be driven by PNA hybridization and peptide-mediated interactions, monitored in situ via temperature-dependent small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) and UV-vis spectroscopy to establish structure-property relationships. Silica encapsulation will be explored to stabilize the resulting heterostructures.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first general design rules for non-DNA-based programmable colloidal crystallization, expanding the toolkit for nanofabrication. It will yield new classes of chemically and thermally robust superlattices, directly impacting nanophotonics by enabling the assembly of perovskite nanocrystals into stable, complex heterostructures with tailored optical properties for next-generation optoelectronics.
proposals.novelty: While PNA and peptides have been used separately in materials science, their integration as a unified, programmable bonding element for colloidal crystal engineering is unprecedented. This approach moves beyond simple DNA replacement by leveraging the synthetic versatility of peptides to introduce new interaction modes and stability profiles not possible with natural nucleic acids.

proposals.title: Programmable Colloidal Crystallization via Shape-Directed DNA Bonding: Using Anisotropic Nanoparticles as Valence-Controlled Building Blocks
proposals.motivation: The field of colloidal crystal engineering, pioneered by DNA-mediated assembly, has largely relied on spherical nanoparticles as isotropic 'atoms,' limiting the structural complexity and functional properties of the resulting superlattices. Pre-2026, the predictive design of crystals with lower symmetry and anisotropic optical or mechanical properties remains a significant challenge. This work addresses a core question in nanoscience: how can we move beyond spherical building blocks to achieve programmable matter with designed symmetry and function?
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the faceted geometry and high aspect ratio of anisotropic nanoparticles (e.g., nanorods, octahedra, or triangular plates) can be exploited as a 'topological' constraint when functionalized with dense shells of DNA, dictating bonding directionality and enabling the predictable assembly of non-close-packed, low-symmetry superlattices not accessible with spheres.
proposals.method: We will synthesize gold nanorods and silica-coated gold octahedra with precise shape control. These will be densely functionalized with thiolated DNA using established methods from the Mirkin group. The DNA sequence design will employ the 'bonding valence' concept, where the spatial distribution of DNA on specific facets is controlled via silica encapsulation and selective deprotection. Assembly will be triggered thermally, and the resulting structures will be characterized by in-situ SAXS, cryo-EM, and tomography to correlate nanoparticle shape and DNA patterning with the final 3D superlattice symmetry and interface structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable framework for using nanoparticle shape as a primary variable to control colloidal crystal symmetry, expanding the library of programmable materials. It will provide fundamental insights into how anisotropic interactions and geometric frustration influence nanoscale assembly, directly addressing unresolved questions in nanoscience. The resulting anisotropic superlattices will serve as new platforms for studying polarized plasmonics and directed energy transfer.
proposals.novelty: While DNA-mediated assembly of spheres is well-known, the deliberate use of non-spherical particle geometry to impose bonding directionality and break isotropic symmetry in DNA-programmed crystals is a non-obvious and underexplored frontier. This moves beyond treating shape as a mere perturbation to leveraging it as a central design element.

proposals.title: Computational Design of DNA Bond Sequences for Programmable Atom Equivalents Using Rosetta-Based Protein Folding Algorithms
proposals.motivation: The assembly of colloidal crystals from programmable atom equivalents (PAEs) relies on the precise engineering of DNA bonds. Current sequence design is largely empirical, limiting the complexity and predictability of the resulting superlattices. Adapting proven computational protein design tools, which excel at predicting molecular interactions and folding, offers a powerful, unexplored route to rationally design DNA sequences for controlling PAE assembly and the resulting material properties.
proposals.hypothesis: That protein design software, specifically the Rosetta framework, can be repurposed to predict the free energy of DNA hybridization and secondary structure formation, enabling the *de novo* design of optimal DNA bond sequences for targeted PAE superlattice symmetry and stability.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Rosetta software suite, a leading tool for protein structure prediction and design, to model single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) and its hybridization. A custom scoring function will be developed, integrating parameters for Watson-Crick base pairing, stacking, and backbone conformation from pre-2026 nucleic acid force fields. We will use Rosetta's Monte Carlo and simulated annealing algorithms to computationally screen and design DNA linker sequences that maximize binding specificity and thermodynamic stability for desired PAE coordination geometries (e.g., BCC, FCC). These sequences will be synthesized and tested on gold nanoparticle PAEs, with assembly characterized by SAXS.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new computational paradigm for the rational design of DNA bonds in nanoscale assembly, moving beyond trial-and-error. It will provide a software framework for predicting and engineering PAE interactions, directly addressing unresolved questions in nanoscience regarding the predictable construction of complex, functional materials from nanoscale building blocks.
proposals.novelty: While Rosetta is extensively used for proteins, its application to the *de novo* design of DNA bonding elements for colloidal crystal engineering is unprecedented. This bridges the distinct fields of computational biophysics (protein folding) and nanomaterial assembly (spherical nucleic acids/PAEs) in a non-obvious way.

proposals.title: Steric Control of Nanocrystal Superlattice Symmetry via Non-Hybridizing DNA Spacers
proposals.motivation: In DNA-programmable nanoparticle assembly, DNA primarily acts as a linker, directing assembly via complementary binding. This limits the accessible crystal symmetries to those defined by DNA hybridization geometries. A method to directly manipulate packing by introducing steric repulsion, rather than just attraction, would significantly expand the library of achievable colloidal crystal structures and their resulting photonic and plasmonic properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by functionalizing nanoparticle surfaces with dense shells of non-complementary, bulky DNA structures (e.g., Y-shaped or dendritic DNA), we can create a tunable steric repulsion that will prevent certain facet-to-facet contacts, thereby forcing nanoparticles to assemble into alternative, non-close-packed crystal symmetries not accessible via linker-based assembly alone.
proposals.method: We will synthesize gold nanoparticles and silica-coated quantum dots and functionalize them with dense monolayers of non-complementary, multi-armed DNA nanostructures (e.g., Y-DNA, X-DNA) using established thiol and silane chemistry. The size, rigidity, and density of these DNA 'spacers' will be systematically varied. Assembly will be induced by the addition of small-molecule dehydrating agents (e.g., PEG), and the resulting superlattices will be characterized by in-situ small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) and electron microscopy. Lattice symmetry and interparticle spacing will be correlated with spacer design using computational models of steric interaction.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm in programmable materials assembly by demonstrating DNA as a steric spacer, not just a linker. It will provide a general design rule for accessing a new class of colloidal crystals with engineered void spaces and symmetries, directly impacting the field of nanophotonics by enabling materials with targeted photonic band gaps. It addresses a core challenge in nanoscience: the precise control of 3D arrangement beyond nearest-neighbor connections.
proposals.novelty: While DNA's role as a programmable 'bond' is well-established, its deliberate use as a non-binding, steric repulsion element to dictate long-range crystal packing is a fundamental inversion of its function in materials science. This approach moves beyond the 'bond valence' model of DNA-mediated assembly to a 'steric valence' model.

proposals.title: Computational Docking of DNA-Programmable Protein Building Blocks to Predict and Design Crystalline Nanomaterials
proposals.motivation: The Mirkin group's spherical nucleic acid (SNA) technology enables the programmable assembly of proteins into crystalline superlattices via DNA hybridization. However, the design rules are largely empirical, and the precise role of protein-protein interactions at the DNA-mediated interface is poorly understood. A predictive computational model for these interactions would accelerate the rational design of protein-based nanomaterials with tailored optical, catalytic, or mechanical properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting and extending established protein-protein docking algorithms to explicitly account for the conformational flexibility and electrostatic environment of DNA linkers will enable accurate prediction of the free energy landscape governing SNA-protein crystal contact formation.
proposals.method: We will adapt the RosettaDock framework, a well-established protein-protein docking algorithm, to model protein-DNA-protein interfaces. Key modifications will include: (1) parameterizing the conformational space of short, flexible ssDNA strands (e.g., poly-T linkers) as part of the docking search; (2) incorporating explicit or implicit solvent models to account for the high-charge density and counterion atmosphere of the DNA backbone; and (3) developing a scoring function that integrates DNA hybridization energy, protein surface complementarity, and electrostatic contributions. Validation will be performed against existing SAXS and crystallographic data from the group's library of SNA-protein superlattices.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first computational pipeline specifically designed for modeling DNA-mediated protein crystallization, moving the field from trial-and-error to predictive design. It will provide fundamental insights into how protein surface chemistry and DNA sequence/structure co-determine lattice symmetry and stability, directly addressing unresolved questions in nanoscience regarding programmable intermolecular interactions.
proposals.novelty: While protein-protein docking and DNA nanotechnology are mature fields, their integration to model the hybrid biomolecular interface of an SNA-protein building block is unprecedented. The novelty lies in treating the DNA not as a rigid spacer but as a flexible, charged, and sequence-programmable component of the docking energy landscape.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Discovery of DNA-Protein Conjugate Crystals via Programmable Droplet Microfluidics
proposals.motivation: The rational design of crystalline materials from DNA-protein conjugates, which combine the programmability of spherical nucleic acids (SNAs) with the rich functionality of proteins, is a major frontier in nanoscience. However, the vast parameter space of conjugate ratios, buffer conditions, and ionic strengths makes identifying crystallization conditions slow and labor-intensive with conventional methods. A high-throughput screening platform is urgently needed to systematically map the phase behavior and accelerate the discovery of novel bio-hybrid crystals with tailored properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating droplet-based microfluidics with real-time optical monitoring will enable the rapid and systematic screening of thousands of DNA-protein conjugate mixing conditions, revealing previously inaccessible crystallization pathways and leading to new classes of structurally ordered bio-hybrid materials.
proposals.method: We will design a PDMS-based droplet microfluidic device to generate monodisperse aqueous droplets containing programmable DNA-functionalized proteins (e.g., streptavidin-DNA conjugates) and complementary DNA-modified nanoparticles or proteins. The droplets will serve as isolated microreactors, with conditions varied via on-chip mixing of streams containing different salts (e.g., MgCl2), pH buffers, and conjugate concentrations. Droplets will be incubated on-chip in delay lines and then analyzed in-line via brightfield and polarized light microscopy to automatically detect crystal formation. Machine learning algorithms (e.g., random forest classification) will be applied to the resulting image and condition datasets to identify key crystallization parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust platform for the high-throughput exploration of DNA-protein conjugate crystallization, generating the first comprehensive maps of their phase behavior. It will directly contribute new, structurally characterized bio-hybrid crystals to the materials library, with potential applications in sensing, catalysis, and photonics. Furthermore, the data-driven approach will yield design rules for programming crystal symmetry and properties through sequence and conjugate architecture.
proposals.novelty: While microfluidics has been used for protein or small-molecule crystallization, its application to the systematic screening of programmable DNA-protein conjugate crystals is unprecedented. The novelty lies in merging Mirkin's expertise in SNA design and protein conjugation with high-throughput microfluidic screening and machine-learning analysis to solve a fundamental bottleneck in bio-nanomaterials discovery.

proposals.title: Site-Specific DNA-Protein Conjugates via Strain-Promoted Azide-Alkyne Cycloaddition for Programmable Nanoscale Architectures
proposals.motivation: The precise conjugation of DNA to proteins is foundational for creating functional biohybrid materials, such as spherical nucleic acid (SNA) architectures and programmable protein assemblies. Current methods, like maleimide-thiol coupling, often suffer from heterogeneity, instability under reducing conditions, and limited control over attachment site, which compromises the structural fidelity and function of the resulting constructs. Bioorthogonal click chemistry offers a promising route to overcome these limitations, enabling robust and site-specific linkages under physiological conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that employing strain-promoted azide-alkyne cycloaddition (SPAAC) between genetically encoded non-canonical amino acids (ncAAs) on a protein surface and azide- or cyclooctyne-modified oligonucleotides will yield monodisperse, stable DNA-protein conjugates with superior control over orientation and valency compared to traditional chemistries.
proposals.method: We will engineer model proteins (e.g., streptavidin, IgG) to incorporate an azidophenylalanine residue at specific sites using amber codon suppression in E. coli. Oligonucleotides will be functionalized with dibenzocyclooctyne (DBCO) via a phosphoramidite synthesis. Conjugation kinetics and efficiency will be optimized by varying buffer conditions and stoichiometry, monitored by gel electrophoresis and mass spectrometry. The resulting conjugates will be assembled into higher-order structures (e.g., crystalline lattices or SNA shells on silica cores) to evaluate their structural integrity and programmable assembly properties via TEM and SAXS.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general, high-fidelity platform for creating well-defined DNA-protein hybrid materials. It will directly advance the field of programmable matter by providing building blocks with predictable interaction motifs, enabling the construction of complex, dynamic nanostructures. The methodology will be a critical tool for applications in sensing, catalysis, and nanophotonics where precise spatial organization is key.
proposals.novelty: While click chemistry has been used for bioconjugation, its systematic application to create site-specific, multivalent DNA-protein conjugates as engineered building blocks for programmable colloidal crystallization and SNA-based materials is novel. This approach merges Mirkin's expertise in DNA-mediated assembly and nanocrystal engineering with precise protein bioengineering, a non-obvious synergy given existing work.

proposals.title: Non-Complementary DNA Linkers for Facet-Selective Nanoparticle Assembly and Anisotropic Superlattice Engineering
proposals.motivation: Spherical nucleic acid (SNA) assembly relies on Watson-Crick complementarity, which typically drives isotropic, close-packed crystallization. However, controlling the orientation of anisotropic nanoparticles (e.g., nanocubes, octahedra) into ordered superlattices with specific facet alignments remains a significant challenge in nanoscience. A method to induce partial, rather than full, interparticle association could unlock new classes of materials with complex interfaces and emergent optical properties, as highlighted in recent work on perovskite heterostructures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deliberately non-complementary DNA sequences, when grafted onto nanoparticle facets, can mediate weak, multivalent interactions sufficient to induce preferential facet alignment during assembly, without driving full isotropic crystallization.
proposals.method: We will synthesize gold nanocubes and octahedra and functionalize specific facets with distinct, non-complementary single-stranded DNA sequences via thiol chemistry. Assembly will be triggered by the addition of a molecular crowding agent (PEG). Using in-situ small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) and cryo-electron microscopy, we will monitor superlattice formation. The DNA interaction landscape will be modeled using coarse-grained molecular dynamics (oxDNA) to predict binding energies and preferred coordination modes of mismatched strands.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design rule for DNA-programmable materials, moving beyond perfect complementarity to exploit weak interactions for structural control. It will provide a generalizable strategy for creating anisotropic nanoparticle superlattices with defined interfacial geometries, directly contributing to the unresolved challenge of constructing complex nanoscale architectures for nanophotonics and catalysis.
proposals.novelty: This approach fundamentally challenges the central dogma of DNA nanotechnology—that specific Watson-Crick pairing is required—by intentionally using non-complementary sequences as structural elements. The novelty lies in using thermodynamic 'frustration' to achieve kinetic trapping in partially aligned states, rather than the global energy minimum of a close-packed structure.

proposals.title: Programming Low-Symmetry Colloidal Crystals via Anisotropic Nanoparticle Facet Matching with DNA
proposals.motivation: The field of DNA-programmable nanoparticle assembly has mastered the creation of highly symmetric, close-packed lattices (e.g., BCC, FCC) from isotropic spheres. However, the assembly of low-symmetry, open lattices—critical for advanced photonic, phononic, and catalytic materials—remains a significant challenge. This project addresses this gap by leveraging the inherent anisotropy of non-spherical nanoparticles and the precise, facet-specific addressability of DNA surface ligands to direct assembly via partial facet interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by selectively functionalizing specific facets of anisotropic nanoparticles (e.g., triangular prisms, octahedra, or concave cubes) with complementary DNA sequences, we can enforce directional bonding through partial facet matching, thereby assembling non-close-packed, low-symmetry colloidal crystals not accessible with spherical building blocks.
proposals.method: We will synthesize gold or silver nanoparticles with controlled anisotropic shapes (e.g., triangular prisms) via seed-mediated growth. Using polyadenine adsorption kinetics and thiolated DNA displacement, we will develop methods to selectively functionalize specific high-index facets. Assembly will be guided by a computational model that maps partial facet contact geometries to potential crystal symmetries, using the 'partial DNA contact' framework. The resulting superlattices will be characterized by synchrotron-based small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) and electron microscopy to determine structure-property relationships, particularly optical anisotropy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general design rulebook for assembling low-symmetry colloidal crystals, expanding the structural landscape of DNA-nanoparticle superlattices. It will directly contribute to solving unresolved questions in nanoscience regarding the control of complex, non-close-packed architectures. The resulting anisotropic metamaterials will provide new platforms for studying directional plasmonic coupling and structure-dependent catalysis.
proposals.novelty: While DNA has been used to assemble spheres and polyhedra into symmetric packings, the deliberate use of partial facet matching on anisotropic shapes to break symmetry and access entirely new lattice classes is a novel and non-obvious extension of the Mirkin group's spherical nucleic acid (SNA) technology.

proposals.title: Programmable Self-Assembly of Nanocrystals via Sequence-Defined Peptoid Foldamers as Shape-Complementary Linkers
proposals.motivation: The predictable self-assembly of nanoscale building blocks into complex, functional materials remains a central challenge in nanoscience. While DNA provides exquisite programmability, its cost, environmental sensitivity, and limited chemical diversity constrain applications in materials science. A synthetic, robust, and chemically versatile linker system is needed to unlock new classes of self-assembled nanomaterials with tailored optical and electronic properties.
proposals.hypothesis: That N-substituted glycine peptoids, with sequences designed to fold into stable secondary structures, can act as programmable, shape-complementary linkers to direct the specific, multivalent assembly of functional nanoparticles into ordered superlattices.
proposals.method: We will synthesize sequence-defined peptoids designed to fold into helices or sheets using solid-phase submonomer synthesis. Key aromatic and aliphatic side chains will be placed to stabilize the fold and present terminal thiol or phosphonate groups for nanoparticle binding (e.g., to Au or metal oxide nanocrystals). Complementary peptoid pairs will be designed using computational modeling (Rosetta or molecular dynamics) to present shape-matched, non-biological interaction motifs (e.g., pi-stacking faces, hydrophobic patches). Assembly will be driven by mixing peptoid-functionalized nanocrystals in solution, with structures characterized by SAXS, TEM, and optical spectroscopy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new, cost-effective platform for programmable nanomaterial assembly orthogonal to biological systems. It will directly demonstrate the use of synthetic foldamers to control nanoscale geometry and interactions, leading to novel superlattices with engineered photonic or catalytic properties. This addresses a key unresolved question in nanotechnology regarding predictable intermolecular coupling beyond DNA.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely merges the sequence-specific programmability of foldamers with the materials-focused utility of nanoparticle self-assembly, moving beyond biological recognition to create entirely synthetic, shape-selective linking rules. This is distinct from prior work using peptides or DNA, offering superior stability and chemical diversity.

proposals.title: Sequence-Defined Synthetic Polymer Linkers for Programmable Nanoparticle Assembly and Enhanced Material Stability
proposals.motivation: Spherical nucleic acids (SNAs) and DNA-mediated assembly are powerful for constructing complex, programmable materials, but DNA's biological origin imposes limitations in cost, enzymatic stability, and environmental robustness. The field of nanoscience lacks a general, scalable framework for using chemically diverse, non-natural polymers as programmable 'smart' linkers that can direct assembly with the same predictability as DNA base pairing. Developing such a platform would expand the chemical and functional space of programmable nanomaterials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sequence-defined synthetic polymers, such as peptoids or oligo(phosphodiester)s, can be designed with orthogonal, sequence-encoded recognition motifs to direct the programmable assembly of nanoparticles into crystalline superlattices, offering enhanced stability and new structure-property relationships compared to DNA-based systems.
proposals.method: We will synthesize sequence-defined peptoid polymers with terminal thiols for gold nanoparticle functionalization, encoding complementary 'shape pairs' via side-chain sterics and hydrogen-bonding motifs. Using high-throughput scanning probe block copolymer lithography, we will fabricate nanoparticle arrays to test linker design rules. Assembly will be guided by computational models (e.g., coarse-grained molecular dynamics) to predict pairing specificity and superlattice symmetry. The structure and optical properties of resulting crystals will be characterized by in-situ small-angle X-ray scattering and UV-vis-NIR spectroscopy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational design rule set for synthetic polymer-based programmable assembly, moving beyond DNA. It will yield new classes of nanoparticle superlattices with tailored optical responses and enhanced thermal/chemical stability, directly addressing unresolved questions in nanoscience regarding robust, scalable bottom-up fabrication.
proposals.novelty: While DNA assembly is well-established, the use of fully synthetic, sequence-defined polymers as programmable linkers for nanoparticle crystallization is unexplored. This approach merges Mirkin's expertise in interactions and SNAs with the design of non-biological, information-encoding polymers to create a new paradigm for materials engineering.

proposals.title: Spatially Programmable Photonic Crystals via Orthogonal, Stimuli-Responsive DNA Linker Domains
proposals.motivation: Colloidal crystals assembled with DNA linkers are powerful platforms for creating tunable photonic materials, but their dynamic reconfiguration is typically global, limiting their utility for complex optical devices. The ability to independently control specific domains within a single crystal lattice would enable the creation of integrated photonic circuits, dynamic optical masks, and reconfigurable metamaterials. This capability is a critical, unmet need in nanophotonics and nanoscience, as highlighted by unresolved questions in the field regarding the dynamic control of nanoscale assemblies.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating multiple orthogonal sets of stimuli-responsive DNA linkers—each sensitive to a distinct, localized trigger such as a specific wavelength of light or a chemical effector—into a single colloidal crystal, we can achieve selective and independent reconfiguration of pre-defined spatial domains, enabling the writing and erasing of complex photonic architectures.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize three orthogonal linker systems: one with photocleavable o-nitrobenzyl groups responsive to 365 nm UV light, a second with azobenzene motifs for reversible cis-trans isomerization under 450 nm blue light, and a third with toehold-mediated strand displacement activated by a specific chemical input. These linkers will be attached to silica-coated gold nanoparticle building blocks. Using dip-pen nanolithography or microfluidic patterning, we will deposit these functionalized particles onto substrates to create crystals with spatially defined linker domains. Reconfiguration will be triggered by focused laser light or localized chemical delivery, with structural changes monitored via in situ dark-field microscopy and small-angle X-ray scattering.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational method for the spatiotemporal programming of colloidal crystal structure and optical properties, moving beyond bulk transformations. It will demonstrate the first proof-of-concept for creating dynamically reconfigurable, heterogeneous photonic architectures such as patterned bandgaps and optical waveguides within a single material. This advances the field towards functional, addressable optical materials for sensing, computing, and information display.
proposals.novelty: While stimuli-responsive DNA linkers exist, their integration into a single crystal for independent, domain-specific control using orthogonal triggers is unprecedented. This approach combines the precision of DNA nanotechnology with the optical functionality of colloidal crystals to achieve a new level of spatiotemporal programmability not possible with existing bulk methods.

proposals.title: Programmable, Stimuli-Responsive Colloidal Crystals via Dynamic DNA Linker Reconfiguration
proposals.motivation: Colloidal crystals assembled via DNA programmable atom equivalents (PAEs) offer exquisite control over lattice symmetry and optical properties. However, these structures are typically static post-assembly. Integrating dynamic, reconfigurable behavior would enable smart materials for adaptive optics, sensing, and tunable photonics, addressing a key frontier in nanoscience for creating next-generation responsive matter.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating stimuli-responsive DNA motifs (e.g., toehold-mediated strand displacement or pH-sensitive i-motifs) as programmable linkers between PAEs will enable the triggered, reversible reconfiguration of colloidal crystal lattices, thereby dynamically modulating their structural and photonic properties.
proposals.method: We will synthesize gold nanoparticle PAEs functionalized with mixed DNA brush layers containing static 'anchor' strands and dynamic 'linker' strands. The dynamic linkers will be designed with specific triggers (complementary DNA fuel strands, pH change). Using in-situ small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) and UV-vis spectroscopy, we will monitor the structural transition from, for example, a body-centered cubic to a simple cubic lattice upon linker deactivation. The kinetics and reversibility will be characterized by cycling the trigger.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general design rulebook for creating dynamically reconfigurable colloidal crystals, moving beyond static assemblies. It will demonstrate direct, in-situ observation of a triggered crystal-crystal phase transition at the nanoscale, contributing fundamental knowledge to both nanoscience and programmable matter. The resulting materials will serve as a platform for tunable optical metamaterials.
proposals.novelty: While DNA has been used to assemble static colloidal crystals, the integration of dynamic DNA nanotechnology to achieve *in-situ*, triggered lattice reconfiguration in 3D crystalline materials is a novel and non-obvious extension of the PAE concept, merging responsive soft matter with hard nanoparticle lattices.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of DNA-Functionalized Patchy Particle Crystals via an Adapted RosettaDock Framework
proposals.motivation: The programmable assembly of colloidal particles into complex, three-dimensional crystals using DNA is a cornerstone of nanomaterial synthesis. However, achieving specific, non-close-packed polyhedral lattices from anisotropic 'patchy' particles remains a significant challenge, as it requires precise control over the valency and spatial orientation of binding interactions. Current design is largely empirical; a predictive computational framework would dramatically accelerate the discovery of new functional colloidal crystals for photonics and catalysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the computational framework for protein-protein docking, which predicts complex 3D assemblies from complementary surface patches, can be successfully adapted to model and inversely design the assembly of DNA-functionalized patchy colloidal particles into target crystal lattices.
proposals.method: We will adapt the RosettaDock algorithm's core sampling and scoring modules. The geometric hashing and FFT-based search will be repurposed to sample configurations of polyhedral nanoparticles with defined DNA 'patch' geometries. The scoring function will be replaced with a hybrid potential integrating: 1) a coarse-grained model for DNA hybridization thermodynamics (e.g., oxDNA parameters), and 2) a term for patch alignment fidelity. This adapted algorithm will be used in an inverse design loop to identify optimal patch arrangements on particle surfaces that stabilize a target lattice (e.g., diamond cubic).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a first-of-its-kind computational design tool for patchy colloidal crystals, moving the field from trial-and-error to predictive design. It will generate specific, testable predictions for DNA strand placement to achieve challenging crystal symmetries, directly addressing unresolved questions in nanoscience regarding controlled hierarchical assembly.
proposals.novelty: The direct conceptual and technical translation of a mature structural biology algorithm (protein docking) to the problem of colloidal crystal engineering is a novel, interdisciplinary approach. It applies the sophisticated sampling and scoring of biomolecular interactions to a materials science problem where analogous 'lock-and-key' recognition is engineered via DNA.

proposals.title: Predictive Design of DNA-Mediated Polyhedral Crystals via Anisotropic Patchy Particle Modeling
proposals.motivation: The Mirkin group's pioneering work on spherical nucleic acids (SNAs) has enabled the programmable assembly of colloidal crystals, but achieving complex, low-symmetry polyhedral architectures remains a significant challenge. Pre-2026, the field lacks robust computational models to predict the thermodynamic stability and kinetic pathways for forming such intricate, DNA-linked superlattices. Integrating concepts from soft matter physics with DNA nanotechnology could unlock a new generation of designer nanomaterials with tailored photonic and catalytic properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling DNA-functionalized nanoparticles as anisotropic patchy particles, with explicit valency and directional binding, will accurately predict the equilibrium phases and assembly conditions for complex polyhedral crystal structures not accessible with isotropic models.
proposals.method: We will employ Monte Carlo (MC) and Molecular Dynamics (MD) simulations using coarse-grained models where nanoparticle cores are represented as polyhedra (e.g., cubes, octahedra) with discrete, mobile DNA-bond patches. The interaction potential will be based on the oxDNA framework to capture sequence-specific hybridization thermodynamics and kinetics. We will systematically vary patch number, geometry, and DNA linker design to compute phase diagrams and identify design rules for targeting specific crystal symmetries, such as diamond or perovskite-like lattices.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a first-principles computational framework for the *a priori* design of DNA-programmed polyhedral colloidal crystals. It will provide specific experimental guidelines for SNA patchiness and linker sequences to realize new metamaterials, directly addressing unresolved questions in nanoscience regarding predictive nanofabrication and complex interface engineering.
proposals.novelty: While patchy particle models exist in soft matter, their application to DNA-mediated assembly has been limited to isotropic spheres. This proposal uniquely combines the oxDNA model with explicit polyhedral patch anisotropy to tackle the non-trivial problem of programming 3D crystal symmetry from building block shape and DNA valency.

proposals.title: Substrate-Registered, DNA Tensegrity Colloidal Crystals for Active Metasurfaces
proposals.motivation: Dynamic colloidal crystals are sought for active photonics and sensing, but achieving large-scale, reversible structural transitions while maintaining precise spatial registry with a substrate remains a major challenge. Pre-2026 work on spherical nucleic acids (SNAs) and DNA-mediated assembly provides exquisite programmability, while lithographic techniques offer positional control, yet these approaches are rarely synergized. Combining them could enable addressable, reconfigurable optical surfaces that are fundamentally inaccessible to static assemblies.
proposals.hypothesis: That a lithographically patterned substrate can template the initial lattice and orientation of colloidal particles, and that interconnecting these particles with a network of tensile, stimuli-responsive DNA linkers will create a substrate-bound tensegrity structure capable of undergoing coherent, large-scale crystal phase transitions in response to specific chemical or thermal triggers.
proposals.method: We will use electron-beam lithography to create arrays of complementary-DNA-functionalized adhesive patches on silica substrates to capture and register specific facets of shape-controlled colloidal particles (e.g., cubes, octahedra). These anchored particles will then be interconnected via a second set of programmable DNA linkers designed to form a tensile network, applying controlled internal stress. The DNA linker sequences will incorporate responsive elements (e.g., i-motifs, toeholds) to trigger reversible contraction or extension, computationally designed to shift the ensemble from a cubic to a tetragonal lattice configuration.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for creating 'active crystalline matter' by fusing top-down lithographic registration with bottom-up tensegrity mechanics. It will yield the first demonstration of a macroscale, substrate-bound colloidal crystal that can reversibly switch between well-defined crystal phases, directly enabling tunable plasmonic or photonic bandgap materials. The project will also advance the fundamental understanding of stress propagation in finite, engineered soft matter systems.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious integration of permanent lithographic registration with transient DNA tensegrity linkages creates a hybrid system where the starting template is fixed, but the superstructure is dynamically reconfigurable, overcoming the typical trade-off between positional order and structural adaptability.

proposals.title: Tensegrity-Inspired Design of DNA-Nanoparticle Superlattices via Minimal, Force-Balanced Interface Engineering
proposals.motivation: The programmable assembly of nanoparticles (NPs) via DNA hybridization is a cornerstone of nanoscience, enabling complex superlattices. However, conventional methods often rely on dense, isotropic DNA functionalization, which is synthetically intensive and can lead to kinetic traps and mechanical fragility. A design paradigm that achieves robust, predictable assembly with minimal DNA use is critically needed to advance the scalability and functional properties of these materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying architectural tensegrity principles—where stability arises from a discontinuous network of rigid struts under compression connected by a continuous network of tensile cables—to DNA-NP assembly will yield superlattices with enhanced mechanical stability and assembly fidelity using a minimal set of strategically placed DNA linkers.
proposals.method: We will computationally model polyhedral gold or silica NPs as compressive struts and single-stranded DNA as tensile cables. Using force-balance algorithms (e.g., adapted from structural engineering or molecular dynamics packages like HOOMD-blue), we will calculate the minimal number and optimal spatial arrangement of DNA attachment points on NP facets to achieve a target lattice symmetry (e.g., BCC, FCC). Experimentally, we will synthesize NPs with site-specific DNA functionalization using known orthogonal chemistry, validate assembly via SAXS/TEM, and probe mechanical properties via nanoindentation of bulk superlattice crystals.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design framework for colloidal crystallization that prioritizes mechanical integrity and synthetic efficiency. It will yield a set of design rules for 'tensegrity DNA-NP lattices' and demonstrate their superior resistance to deformation and thermal disassembly compared to fully functionalized counterparts, directly addressing unresolved questions in nanoscience regarding robust nanoscale construction.
proposals.novelty: While tensegrity is known in macroscale architecture and has been explored in protein structures, its application to guide the interface design in programmable nanoparticle assembly is unprecedented. This moves beyond empirical or isotropic functionalization to a first-principles, mechanics-driven design of the bonding network.

proposals.title: Programming Atomic and Molecular Assembly via Shape-Complementary Pairing for the Rational Design of Bulk Crystals
proposals.motivation: The rational design of bulk crystalline materials from first principles remains a grand challenge in materials science. While the Mirkin group's spherical nucleic acid (SNA) technology has masterfully programmed colloidal crystal formation via shape-complementary interactions, translating this powerful assembly paradigm to the atomic and molecular scale could unlock a new frontier in predictive crystal engineering. This addresses a core unresolved question in nanoscience: how to achieve precise, bottom-up control over solid-state structure and its resulting optical and electronic properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the conceptual framework of shape-complementary, programmable interactions—successful for nanoscale assembly—can be scaled down to guide the co-crystallization of atomic ions and small molecules, enabling the synthesis of novel bulk crystalline phases with targeted properties.
proposals.method: We will employ a computational design-first approach, using density functional theory (DFT) to calculate the electron density surfaces and electrostatic potentials of target atomic ions (e.g., complex perovskite A-cations) and small organic linker molecules. Custom algorithms will be developed to identify and score shape-complementary pairing motifs. Promising pairs will be synthesized using solution-phase and vapor-transport methods, where the 'programming' is achieved by functionalizing molecular linkers with complementary hydrogen-bonding or dipole-directing groups. The resulting crystals will be characterized via single-crystal X-ray diffraction, and their optical properties (e.g., photoluminescence) will be correlated with the designed interfacial structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new methodology for the *ab initio* design of bulk crystalline materials, moving beyond traditional trial-and-error synthesis. It will directly contribute to the field of nanofabrication for nanophotonics by creating crystals with engineered complex interfaces and predictable optoelectronic properties. The project will generate a set of design rules for atomic/molecular shape complementarity, analogous to the rules governing SNA assembly.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a biomimetic, programmable assembly concept—validated at the nanoscale—to the fundamental building blocks of matter (atoms and small molecules), a leap in scale that is non-obvious and has not been systematically explored for bulk crystal design.

proposals.title: Programmable DNA-Mediated Tessellation of Flexible Lipid Membranes for Tunable Compartmentalization and Signaling
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the field of DNA nanotechnology excels at creating rigid, solid 3D crystals and polyhedra, but lacks robust methods for engineering soft, flexible, and dynamic membrane architectures. Lipid vesicles and protein cages are fundamental to cellular compartmentalization and signaling, yet their functionalization is typically stochastic. A method to impose precise, programmable 2D order on these soft interfaces would unlock new paradigms in synthetic biology and responsive materials.
proposals.hypothesis: Spherical nucleic acid (SNA) architectures, where dense DNA shells are conjugated to lipid or protein nanocages, can be designed to direct the tessellation of these flexible shells via sequence-specific hybridization, thereby creating membranes with predictable, addressable lattice geometries that modulate mechanical and transport properties.
proposals.method: We will employ Chad Mirkin's core SNA technology, conjugating dense monolayers of thiolated DNA to maleimide-functionalized liposomes and engineered protein cages (e.g., ferritin). Using the 'bonds-in-rotator' design principle from colloidal crystal engineering, we will design orthogonal DNA linker sets to drive the directional assembly of these functionalized shells into extended 2D membrane sheets or finite closed vesicles with tessellated patterns (e.g., hexagonal or kagome lattices). Assembly will be characterized in situ via cryo-electron tomography and monitored for dynamics using FRET-paired DNA strands on adjacent shells.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a first-of-its-kind platform for the 'crystalline' patterning of soft matter interfaces, providing fundamental insights into how programmed interactions govern the mesoscale order of flexible shells. It will deliver a new class of biohybrid materials with engineered porosity and valency for applications in multiplexed sensing, controlled drug release, and as minimal cell models with spatially organized surface reactions.
proposals.novelty: While DNA has been used to assemble rigid nanoparticles and liposomes into clusters or crystals, the deliberate tessellation of the *membrane itself* into a well-defined 2D lattice via SNA-mediated interactions on flexible polyhedral shells is a novel and unexplored design space at the intersection of supramolecular chemistry and soft matter physics.

proposals.title: Computational Design of Anisotropic Nanoparticle Assemblies via Adaptation of Protein-Protein Docking Algorithms
proposals.motivation: The programmable assembly of anisotropic nanoparticles into complex, functional architectures is a central challenge in nanoscience, with direct implications for nanophotonics, catalysis, and sensing. Current design strategies are largely empirical, lacking predictive computational tools for modeling the complex, orientation-dependent interactions between non-spherical nanoscale objects, which limits the rational design of materials with targeted properties.
proposals.hypothesis: That the geometric and energetic scoring functions from established protein-protein docking algorithms (e.g., RosettaDock, ZDOCK) can be adapted and parameterized to accurately predict the binding modes and free energies of anisotropic nanoparticles functionalized with DNA or protein shells, enabling the computational design of specific superlattice structures.
proposals.method: We will adapt the RosettaDock framework, replacing atomistic protein side chains with coarse-grained models of DNA strands or proteins densely grafted onto anisotropic nanoparticle cores (e.g., rods, plates, cubes). The algorithm's rigid-body search and side-chain optimization steps will be modified to sample nanoparticle orientations and ligand conformations. Interaction potentials will be parameterized using experimental data from spherical nucleic acid (SNA) assembly and pre-2026 computational studies of DNA-mediated nanoparticle interactions to create a 'nanoparticle force field'.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a first-of-its-kind computational pipeline for the *ab initio* prediction and design of anisotropic nanoparticle superlattices, moving the field beyond trial-and-error. It will directly address unresolved questions in nanoscience regarding the control of complex interfaces and structure-property relationships in heterostructured materials.
proposals.novelty: While protein docking is mature and SNA assembly is well-studied, the explicit adaptation of these algorithms' rigorous sampling and scoring methodologies to engineer interactions between inorganic, anisotropic nanoscale objects is a novel, non-obvious fusion of biophysical computation and inorganic nanomaterials design.

proposals.title: Catalytic Capping Agents for Kinetically Sculpted Nanocrystal Morphologies: A Combined Computational and Experimental Framework
proposals.motivation: Controlling nanoparticle morphology is central to tailoring properties for catalysis, photonics, and sensing. Current capping agents primarily stabilize thermodynamic shapes, limiting access to kinetically trapped morphologies with potentially superior properties. A predictive framework for active, kinetic control via capping agents remains an unresolved challenge in nanoscience, as highlighted in recent assessments of the field.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that molecular capping agents can be rationally designed to not only bind selectively to specific crystal facets but also to catalyze surface reactions that accelerate the growth or dissolution of those facets, enabling programmable kinetic control over nanoparticle morphology.
proposals.method: We will employ density functional theory (DFT) to screen libraries of organic ligands and polymers for their binding energy and ability to lower activation barriers for surface reactions (e.g., metal ion reduction or complexation) on specific facets of model systems like gold or halide perovskite nanocrystals. Promising 'catalytic cappers' will be synthesized and their action monitored in situ using liquid-phase transmission electron microscopy (LP-TEM) and time-resolved X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) during nanoparticle synthesis. This will validate the proposed kinetic mechanisms and establish structure-property relationships.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of functional capping agents that act as kinetic directors, expanding the library of accessible nanoparticle morphologies. It will provide a generalizable, predictive framework linking capper molecular design to dynamic surface processes and final nanostructure, directly addressing the need for advanced nanofabrication tools.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving beyond the equilibrium, passivating role of cappers to an active, catalytic function that manipulates growth kinetics, a concept distinct from conventional shape-control strategies. The integration of computational screening for catalytic activity with real-time, in situ experimental validation of kinetic pathways is a transformative approach.

proposals.title: Selective Nanoparticle Morphogenesis via Competitive Destabilization: An Enzymatic Inhibition Analogy for Facet-Specific Etching
proposals.motivation: Precise control over nanoparticle morphology is critical for tailoring optical, catalytic, and electronic properties, yet synthetic strategies remain dominated by kinetic growth control and passive facet stabilization. A fundamental gap exists in the ability to *actively* and selectively destabilize specific crystallographic facets during synthesis, limiting access to novel, non-equilibrium nanostructures. This project addresses this by inverting the traditional role of capping agents, inspired by the specificity of competitive inhibition in enzymology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that capping agents can be rationally designed to act not as stabilizers, but as facet-specific destabilizing agents. By engineering molecular head groups for high-affinity, selective binding to a target facet (e.g., {100} of a metal or perovskite), these agents will introduce localized lattice strain or electrostatic repulsion, rendering that facet susceptible to preferential etching or inhibiting its growth during synthesis.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined computational and experimental approach. First, density functional theory (DFT) calculations will screen custom-designed ligand libraries (e.g., modified thiols, phosphonates, or cationic polymers) for binding energy differences and predicted lattice strain induction on gold and CsPbBr3 perovskite {100} vs. {111} facets. Selected candidates will be synthesized and tested in wet-chemical syntheses. For gold, we will use a standard seed-mediated growth in the presence of the destabilizing agent and a mild etchant (e.g., I⁻/O₂). For perovskites, we will introduce the agent during ligand-assisted reprecipitation. Morphological evolution will be tracked via TEM, high-resolution SEM, and XRD to correlate agent structure with facet-specific etching outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm in nanomaterial synthesis by introducing the concept of active, selective destabilization, moving beyond passive stabilization. It will provide a generalizable design framework for 'competitive inhibitor' capping agents, enabling the creation of nanoparticle morphologies inaccessible through conventional growth methods. The resulting structures will be tested for enhanced catalytic activity and tunable plasmonic/photonic properties.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the conceptual inversion of a capping agent's role from stabilizer to selective destabilizer, a direct analogy to competitive inhibition not previously applied to nanoparticle morphology control. This approach is distinct from using etching agents alone, as the specificity is encoded in the designed ligand-facet interaction, not the etchant's inherent chemistry.

proposals.title: Programmable Facet Engineering in Bimetallic Nanoparticles via DNA-Encoded Biomimetic Regulators
proposals.motivation: The catalytic and plasmonic properties of alloy nanoparticles are critically dependent on their exposed crystallographic facets, yet achieving precise, independent control over facet identity and elemental composition in multi-metallic systems remains a major synthetic challenge. Drawing inspiration from biomineralization, where organic matrices direct inorganic crystal growth, offers a powerful but underexplored route to such control. This work addresses a key unresolved question in nanoscience: how to programmably dictate structure at complex inorganic interfaces.
proposals.hypothesis: That sequence-defined DNA or peptide nucleic acid (PNA) constructs can be designed as synthetic biomimetic regulators to selectively bind to specific crystallographic planes of a seed nanoparticle, thereby kinetically steering the site-specific deposition of a secondary metal to create alloy shells with designer exposed facets.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-step guided regulation framework. First, polyhedral metal nanocrystals (e.g., Pd, Au) will be synthesized as seeds. Second, a library of thiolated DNA or PNA sequences, selected for predicted binding affinity to specific metal facets via molecular dynamics simulations, will be introduced. These regulators will be integrated into a slow-precursor-injection synthesis, where their selective adsorption will modulate the reduction kinetics and deposition sites of a secondary metal (e.g., Pt, Ag), directing alloy shell formation. Products will be characterized by high-resolution TEM, XRD, and EDS mapping to correlate regulator sequence with final facet composition and architecture.
proposals.contribution: This will establish a new biomimetic design rule for the synthesis of complex multi-metallic nanoparticles with programmable interfacial structures. It will yield a versatile toolkit for creating catalysts with optimized active sites and plasmonic particles with tailored hot-spot geometries. The work will directly bridge the fields of biomineralization, DNA nanotechnology, and inorganic nanosynthesis.
proposals.novelty: While biomolecules have been used to nucleate or shape simple nanoparticles, their application as sequence-specific facet-selective regulators to guide *alloying* processes in pre-formed nanocrystals is novel. This moves beyond passive templating to active, kinetic control of heterogeneous metal deposition at the atomic scale.

proposals.title: Continuous-Flow Synthesis of Spherical Nucleic Acid Architectures via a Guided Regulation Framework
proposals.motivation: The batch synthesis of complex, multi-component nanostructures like spherical nucleic acids (SNAs) limits reproducibility and scalability, hindering their translation into clinical and industrial applications. Continuous flow synthesis offers superior control over reaction kinetics and mixing, enabling precise regulation of nanoparticle properties, but adapting the intricate, multi-step assembly of SNAs to a continuous process remains a significant challenge. This work addresses a critical bottleneck in nanomanufacturing identified in recent assessments of unresolved questions in nanoscience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the principles of the guided regulation framework—which uses programmable DNA interactions to control the assembly and structure of nanomaterials—can be translated from batch to a modular continuous-flow reactor system to produce monodisperse SNAs with enhanced structural control and yield.
proposals.method: We will design a modular flow reactor with sequential zones for silica nanoparticle core formation, surface functionalization, and stepwise DNA hybridization, informed by Mirkin's expertise in SNA design and silica materials. Real-time in-line monitoring (e.g., UV-Vis, DLS) will provide feedback to a control algorithm that adjusts flow rates, temperature, and reagent introduction to maintain optimal assembly conditions, directly applying the 'guided regulation' concept dynamically. The system will be tested for synthesizing SNAs with varied DNA densities and sequences to probe the structure-property relationships of the continuously produced architectures.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational platform for the scalable, reproducible production of sophisticated nucleic acid-based nanomaterials, directly impacting fields from nanophotonics to therapeutics. It will generate new insights into the kinetics of interfacial biomolecular assembly under flow conditions, contributing to the fundamental science of complex nanomaterial synthesis.
proposals.novelty: While continuous flow synthesis of simple nanoparticles is established, applying a dynamic, feedback-guided framework to the multi-step, interaction-driven assembly of bio-inorganic hybrid structures like SNAs is a novel and non-obvious integration of Mirkin's core methodologies. This moves beyond batch process optimization to create an intelligent manufacturing system for programmable nanomaterials.

proposals.title: Spherical Nucleic Acid (SNA) Architectures for the Targeted Depletion of Immunosuppressive Myeloid-Derived Suppressor Cells (MDSCs) in Solid Tumors
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in cancer immunotherapy, many solid tumors remain resistant due to a highly immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment (TME). A key barrier is the prevalence of myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs), which actively inhibit T-cell function and promote tumor progression. Pre-2026 nanoparticle strategies have predominantly focused on activating antigen-presenting cells (APCs), but a paradigm shift towards directly depleting or reprogramming these immunosuppressive populations is critically needed to overcome resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that SNA constructs, functionalized with specific aptamers targeting MDSC surface markers (e.g., Gr-1, S100A8/A9), can be engineered to selectively deliver gene-regulatory or cytotoxic payloads to MDSCs, leading to their depletion and a consequent reversion of local immunosuppression in the TME.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize core-shell silica nanoparticles as a robust, high-surface-area platform for dense SNA shell functionalization. Using a combinatorial screening approach, we will identify optimal DNA aptamer sequences for murine and human MDSC targeting. These aptamers will be conjugated to the SNA shell to confer specificity. The SNA constructs will be loaded with either siRNA against key survival genes (e.g., STAT3) or a photosensitizer for photothermal ablation. We will characterize the structure-property relationships of these constructs (size, loading, stability) and test their efficacy in vitro using MDSC cell lines and primary cells, followed by evaluation in murine solid tumor models.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of SNA-based nanotherapeutics specifically engineered for immunosuppressive cell targeting, moving beyond traditional APC-focused immunomodulation. It will provide fundamental insights into how nanoparticle architecture and surface chemistry influence interactions with suppressive myeloid cells. The project aims to deliver a potent, targeted tool for reshaping the TME to enhance the efficacy of existing immunotherapies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the canonical immunostimulatory focus of SNAs to instead target and eliminate immunosuppressive cells, a relatively unexplored application for this platform. Furthermore, the integration of MDSC-specific aptamers with the dense, oriented oligonucleotide shell of SNAs creates a unique multivalent targeting system not achievable with conventional linear oligonucleotide conjugates.

proposals.title: Systematic Investigation of Valency in Spherical Nucleic Acid (SNA) Architectures: Defining the Trade-off Between Binding Efficacy and Specificity
proposals.motivation: Multivalency is a cornerstone of supramolecular chemistry and nanomedicine, exploited in Chad Mirkin's pioneering SNA work to enhance binding avidity. However, the field's assumption that higher valency is universally optimal may overlook significant drawbacks, including increased off-target binding and immunogenicity, which limit therapeutic specificity and safety. A systematic study to define the precise relationship between ligand valency, binding efficacy, and specificity is critically needed to inform the rational design of next-generation nanocarriers and diagnostic probes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a given SNA core size and ligand-receptor pair, an optimal, intermediate valency exists that maximizes target engagement while minimizing non-specific interactions, and that this optimum can be predicted from the structural and thermodynamic parameters of the system.
proposals.method: We will employ a modular DNA-based synthesis platform to fabricate gold nanoparticle (AuNP) SNAs with precise, tunable valencies (1, 2, 4, 8, 16) of a model protein-binding aptamer or antibody fragment. Using surface plasmon resonance (SPR) and fluorescence anisotropy, we will measure binding kinetics (kon, koff, KD) to both target and structurally similar off-target proteins. High-resolution structural analysis via cryo-electron microscopy (cryo-EM) and small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) will correlate nanostructure with function. A computational model integrating these data will be developed to predict optimal valency based on receptor density and binding free energy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational design principle for valency in nanoscale targeting systems, moving beyond the 'more is better' heuristic. It will provide a quantitative framework to engineer SNAs and related nanostructures for applications requiring high specificity, such as in vivo diagnostics and targeted drug delivery to cells with low receptor expression, thereby enhancing therapeutic indices.
proposals.novelty: While multivalency is well-studied, a deliberate, systematic deconstruction of valency to find a potential 'sweet spot' for specificity in programmable SNA architectures is a non-obvious and underexplored inverse approach. Combining precise nanofabrication with high-resolution structural biology to solve this optimization problem represents a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: Programmable Spherical Nucleic Acid Architectures as Dynamic, Structure-Adapting Nanotherapeutics for the Tumor Microenvironment
proposals.motivation: The tumor microenvironment (TME) is a dynamic, heterogeneous, and immunosuppressive landscape that presents a major barrier to effective nanotherapeutic delivery and action. Pre-2026 nanomedicine has largely focused on static, homogeneous nanoparticles, which often fail to adapt to evolving TME conditions like variable pH, enzyme activity, and cellular composition. A materials-based strategy to create nanoparticles that can sense and structurally reconfigure in response to specific TME cues is critically needed to improve targeting, penetration, and therapeutic efficacy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that spherical nucleic acid (SNA) constructs, engineered with programmable, conditional assembly domains, will undergo predictable structural reconfiguration (e.g., from a core-shell to a disassembled or heterogeneously aggregated state) in response to TME-specific stimuli, leading to enhanced tumor penetration and cell-specific uptake compared to static nanostructures.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize two classes of SNAs: 1) 'Heterogeneous' SNAs featuring a gold nanoparticle core functionalized with a dense shell of oligonucleotides bearing pH-labile (e.g., i-motif) or enzyme-cleavable linkers, and 2) 'Dynamic' SNAs comprising two populations of functionalized nanoparticles (e.g., gold and silica) programmed for conditional, stimulus-driven hybridization and assembly. Using Mirkin's established SNA synthesis and DNA programmable assembly techniques, we will control interparticle distance and valence. Structural changes will be characterized in vitro using dynamic light scattering, TEM, and optical property shifts. Efficacy will be tested in 3D tumor spheroid models, quantifying penetration depth and cellular internalization via confocal microscopy and flow cytometry.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for 'smart' nanotherapeutics that actively adapt their physical structure to environmental cues. It will provide a direct, head-to-head comparison of heterogeneous versus dynamically assembling nanoparticle strategies for TME targeting. The findings will contribute a novel, structurally defined platform to the field of nanomedicine, moving beyond passive delivery toward adaptive materials.
proposals.novelty: While SNAs are established as static delivery vehicles, their use as dynamically reconfigurable, stimulus-responsive architectures that leverage their inherent programmability for structural adaptation in the TME is unexplored. This approach uniquely combines Mirkin's expertise in precise nucleic acid-based materials design with the unresolved challenge of TME adaptability highlighted in pre-2026 nanoscience roadmaps.

proposals.title: Spherical Nucleic Acid (SNA)-Mediated Adjuvant Delivery to Laser-Induced Thermal Lesions for Post-Ablation Immunomodulation
proposals.motivation: Laser interstitial thermal therapy (LITT) is a minimally invasive surgical technique for treating solid tumors, but local recurrence and metastatic spread remain significant challenges. Pre-2026 research indicates that thermal ablation can release tumor antigens but often fails to generate a robust, systemic anti-tumor immune response. Combining ablation with immunomodulatory nanotherapies presents a promising strategy to convert the ablation site into an in situ vaccine depot.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that functionalizing silica-coated gold nanoparticles, used as LITT sensitizers, with adjuvant-loaded Spherical Nucleic Acids (SNAs) will create a localized immunogenic microenvironment post-ablation, enhancing antigen presentation and generating a systemic, tumor-specific cytotoxic T-cell response.
proposals.method: We will synthesize core-shell nanoparticles with a gold nanorod core (for LITT sensitization) and a mesoporous silica shell. The silica surface will be densely functionalized with SNAs, where oligonucleotide shells are designed to carry immunostimulatory CpG sequences. Using Mirkin's established SNA design rules, we will optimize structure and density for stability and cellular uptake. Post-LITT in murine glioma models, we will quantify immune cell infiltration (via flow cytometry), systemic T-cell responses, and abscopal effects on distant tumors.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel materials platform that physically and functionally integrates thermal ablation with nanotechnology-based immunotherapy. It will provide fundamental insights into how nanoscale architecture and presentation of immunomodulators at the ablation cavity interface influence anti-tumor immunity, moving beyond simple co-administration strategies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the covalent integration of SNA-based adjuvants directly onto the thermal sensitizing nanoparticle, creating a single, multifunctional agent that performs ablation and immediate, localized immunomodulation, a concept distinct from sequential delivery of separate components.

proposals.title: Spherical Nucleic Acid (SNA) Nanostructures for the Co-Delivery of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors and Tumor-Associated Antigens to Modulate Systemic Anti-Tumor Immunity
proposals.motivation: Immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI) therapies, such as anti-PD-1/PD-L1 antibodies, have revolutionized oncology but suffer from low response rates and immune-related adverse events due to systemic administration. Pre-2026, nanoscale delivery systems, particularly Chad Mirkin's SNAs, have shown exceptional promise for targeted intracellular delivery of nucleic acids and proteins, but their potential to synergistically co-deliver ICIs with other immunomodulatory cargo to specific immune cell populations remains largely unexplored. This represents a critical opportunity to enhance therapeutic efficacy while minimizing systemic toxicity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that SNA architectures, functionalized with both a monoclonal antibody targeting the PD-1 checkpoint and a tumor-associated antigen (TAA) peptide or mRNA, will enable targeted, dual-action modulation of antigen-presenting cells and T-cells, leading to a more potent and specific systemic anti-tumor immune response than either agent delivered alone or systemically.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize core-shell SNA nanostructures using a gold nanoparticle core. The SNA shell will consist of oligonucleotides hybridized with strands conjugated to: 1) a Fab fragment or full IgG of an anti-PD-1 antibody, and 2) a model TAA peptide (e.g., ovalbumin SIINFEKL) or its encoding mRNA. We will employ established silica-coating and bioconjugation techniques from the Mirkin group to control valency and orientation. The structures will be characterized for size, stability, and binding affinity. Their immunomodulatory function will be tested in vitro using dendritic cell and T-cell co-cultures, and in vivo in a syngeneic B16-OVA melanoma model, monitoring tumor growth, immune cell infiltration, and systemic cytokine profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel platform for the combinatorial delivery of protein and nucleic acid immunotherapeutics within a single, defined nanostructure. It will provide fundamental insights into how nanoscale presentation of ICIs alongside antigens influences immune synapse formation and T-cell priming. The project aims to demonstrate a new strategy to convert 'cold' tumors to 'hot,' potentially improving the therapeutic index of existing immunotherapies.
proposals.novelty: While SNAs have delivered nucleic acids and antibodies separately, their use as a precision scaffold to co-present a checkpoint inhibitor and a specific antigen in a spatially controlled manner to simultaneously engage multiple immune pathways is a novel and non-obvious integration of Mirkin's core SNA technology with systemic immunotherapy.

proposals.title: De Novo Computational Design of Spherical Nucleic Acid Architectures for Programmable Immune Receptor Activation
proposals.motivation: The rational design of nanoparticle surfaces for specific biological targeting remains a major challenge in nanomedicine. While Chad Mirkin's lab has pioneered Spherical Nucleic Acids (SNAs) as programmable scaffolds, the precise, atom-level control over protein corona formation and immune receptor engagement is largely empirical. Computational protein design offers a path to engineer these interactions from first principles, enabling the creation of next-generation immunomodulatory materials.
proposals.hypothesis: That Rosetta-based protein design algorithms can be used to generate de novo peptide or protein sequences which, when displayed on a gold nanoparticle core via SNA chemistry, will fold into stable structures that selectively and predictably engage specific immune cell receptors, such as Toll-like receptors (TLRs) or scavenger receptors.
proposals.method: We will use the Rosetta software suite (pre-2026 versions) for computational protein design. Starting with a model of a gold nanoparticle densely functionalized with oligonucleotides, we will treat the terminal region of the DNA as a 'scaffold' for designing structured peptide loops or miniproteins. Using Rosetta's fixed-backbone design and fold-and-dock protocols, we will generate sequences predicted to fold into stable conformations that present specific epitopes for target receptors. High-scoring designs will be synthesized as DNA-peptide conjugates, assembled into SNAs, and characterized for structure (CD, TEM) and receptor-binding affinity (SPR, cell-based assays).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a first-of-its-kind computational pipeline for the atomistic design of bio-nano interfaces. It will yield a new class of 'designed protein corona' SNAs with precisely defined immunostimulatory properties, moving beyond simple sequence-based targeting. The results will provide fundamental insights into how synthetic nanomaterials can be engineered to mimic natural protein-receptor interactions.
proposals.novelty: While computational protein design has been applied to soluble proteins and surfaces, its direct application to engineer the complex, crowded, and curved interface of a spherical nucleic acid nanoparticle for immune receptor engagement is unprecedented. This merges Mirkin's expertise in SNA materials science with state-of-the-art (pre-2026) computational biology in a novel way.

proposals.title: Spherical Nucleic Acid-Inspired, Ionizable Lipid Nanoparticles for the Co-Delivery of Glioblastoma Antigen mRNA Cocktails to Tumor-Associated Myeloid Cells
proposals.motivation: Glioblastoma (GBM) remains a lethal cancer with poor survival, partly due to its immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment (TME). Antigen-presenting cells (APCs) within the TME, such as macrophages and dendritic cells, are often dysfunctional. While mRNA-LNP vaccines have shown clinical success for infectious diseases and some cancers, their application for reprogramming intratumoral APCs in GBM using multi-antigen payloads is underexplored. This represents a critical unmet need for in situ vaccination strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an LNP platform, engineered with surface and structural properties inspired by spherical nucleic acids (SNAs) to enhance interactions with myeloid cells, can efficiently co-deliver a rationally selected cocktail of GBM-associated antigen mRNAs to APCs in the TME, thereby reversing local immunosuppression and stimulating potent anti-tumor immunity.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of ionizable lipids and formulate LNPs using microfluidic mixing, systematically varying lipid composition and polyethylene glycol (PEG)-lipid density to modulate surface properties and protein corona. Inspired by SNA design principles, we will functionalize LNP surfaces with precisely oriented, dense oligonucleotide shells to target scavenger receptors on APCs. LNPs will be loaded with mRNA encoding a cocktail of GBM neoantigens and immunomodulators. We will characterize LNP structure-property relationships using SAXS and cryo-EM, assess APC uptake and antigen presentation in vitro using primary human myeloid cells, and evaluate efficacy in orthotopic murine GBM models via flow cytometry of the TME and survival studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of nucleic acid delivery materials that merge the clinical advantages of LNPs with the programmable targeting and dense presentation of SNAs. It will provide a fundamental understanding of how LNP surface architecture influences interactions with specific immune cell subsets in the TME. The project will yield a potent, locally administered platform for in situ vaccination against solid tumors.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate fusion of the clinically validated LNP framework with Mirkin's SNA design rules—specifically, the dense, oriented nucleic acid shell—to create a new material specifically for reprogramming intratumoral APCs, rather than systemic vaccination.
