This approach moves beyond using LNPs as simple carriers to engineering them as synthetic, interactive immune-engaging particles.

proposals.title: Spherical Nucleic Acid (SNA) Architectures with Integrated Synthetic Gene Circuits for Tumor Microenvironment-Responsive Drug Delivery
proposals.motivation: Current nanoparticle-based cancer therapies often lack precise spatiotemporal control, leading to off-target effects and suboptimal efficacy. The tumor microenvironment (TME) presents unique biochemical signals (e.g., hypoxia, specific proteases, low pH) that could be harnessed for conditional activation. Integrating the programmability of synthetic biology with the robust delivery and biodistribution properties of engineered nanomaterials represents a major frontier in targeted therapeutics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by encapsulating cell-free synthetic gene circuits within the core of a silica-coated SNA, we can create a nanoparticle that transduces specific TME signals (e.g., elevated MMP-9 protease) into the conditional production and release of a protein-based therapeutic payload.
proposals.method: We will design toehold switch-based gene circuits responsive to TME-specific RNA triggers or protease-cleavable transcriptional repressors. These circuits, encoding a therapeutic protein (e.g., granzyme B), will be encapsulated within a porous silica nanoparticle core via a sol-gel process. The core will be densely functionalized with a shell of oligonucleotides to form an SNA, conferring cellular uptake and stability. The silica matrix will be engineered with peptide linkers cleavable by TME proteases (e.g., MMP-9) to control circuit accessibility. Particle structure, conditional protein expression, and cytotoxicity will be characterized in 3D tumor spheroid models.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of 'smart' nanomaterial that merges the sensing and logic-gating capabilities of synthetic biology with the favorable pharmacological properties of SNAs. It will provide a generalizable platform for translating specific molecular signatures into therapeutic actions directly at the disease site, moving beyond simple ligand-receptor targeting.
proposals.novelty: While synthetic gene circuits and SNAs have been developed independently, their integration into a single, signal-responsive nanodevice is unexplored. The novelty lies in using the SNA architecture not just for delivery, but as an integral component that gates access to a encapsulated, cell-free synthetic circuit based on the TME state.

proposals.title: Bioorthogonal Click Assembly of Spherical Nucleic Acid (SNA) Architectures within the Glioblastoma Microenvironment for Targeted Therapy
proposals.motivation: Glioblastoma (GBM) remains a lethal brain cancer with a poor prognosis, largely due to the inability to deliver sufficient therapeutic doses across the blood-brain barrier and into the complex tumor microenvironment (TME). Pre-2026, Chad Mirkin's lab pioneered Spherical Nucleic Acids (SNAs) as potent, modular structures for gene regulation and immunotherapy, but their systemic delivery to solid tumors like GBM faces challenges with biodistribution and off-target effects. Bioorthogonal chemistry, which enables specific, catalyst-free reactions in living systems, offers a promising strategy to overcome these barriers by constructing therapeutics in situ.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that intravenously injected, bioorthogonally functionalized SNA core components and therapeutic oligonucleotide cargos will selectively undergo click assembly within the GBM TME, leading to enhanced local accumulation, potent target gene knockdown, and improved therapeutic efficacy with reduced systemic toxicity.
proposals.method: We will design two distinct, non-interacting nanoparticle components: (1) a gold nanoparticle core functionalized with trans-cyclooctene (TCO)-modified DNA, and (2) a silica-coated therapeutic oligonucleotide cargo (e.g., anti-BCL2 siRNA) functionalized with tetrazine (Tz). Using pre-2026 SNA design principles, we will optimize the density and orientation of these bioorthogonal handles. Following systemic co-injection into orthotopic GBM mouse models, the rapid, specific, and catalyst-free inverse electron-demand Diels-Alder (IEDDA) reaction between TCO and Tz will drive the in vivo assembly of complete, therapeutic SNAs exclusively within the tumor, which we will monitor via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) pairs and quantify using ICP-MS and confocal microscopy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel platform for the spatially controlled, in vivo synthesis of complex nanotherapeutics, directly extending the utility of SNAs to previously challenging disease sites like GBM. It will provide fundamental insights into the kinetics and fidelity of bioorthogonal reactions in complex tissue microenvironments and demonstrate a new paradigm for achieving high local therapeutic indices through targeted supramolecular assembly.
proposals.novelty: While bioorthogonal chemistry has been used for small molecule conjugation and pretargeted imaging, its application for the programmable, multi-component assembly of entire functional nanostructures like SNAs inside a solid tumor is unprecedented. This merges Mirkin's expertise in SNA design and nanofabrication with the precision of bioorthogonal reactions to solve a critical delivery problem in oncology.

proposals.title: Spherical Nucleic Acid (SNA)-Encoded Nanoparticles for Single-Cell Phenotype-Specific Targeting and Signaling in the Tumor Microenvironment
proposals.motivation: The tumor microenvironment (TME) contains rare, phenotypically distinct immune cell subsets that critically influence therapeutic outcomes, but current nanocarriers lack the specificity to engage them individually. Pre-2026 work shows that nanoparticle properties like size, shape, and surface chemistry dictate biodistribution, but targeting remains largely organ or broad cell-type specific. Achieving single-cell-subset precision within the complex TME is a major unresolved challenge in nanomedicine and immunotherapy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by precisely engineering the density, orientation, and sequence of targeting ligands on a nanoparticle core using a spherical nucleic acid (SNA) architecture, we can create a library of particles with distinct avidity and selectivity profiles capable of delivering unique optical or genetic signals to specific, rare immune cell subsets (e.g., T-regulatory vs. exhausted CD8+ T cells).
proposals.method: We will synthesize a core-shell nanoparticle library with gold or silica cores and a dense shell of radially oriented DNA, forming the SNA. Using a modular design, we will conjugate distinct protein-binding aptamers or engineered antibody fragments (e.g., scFvs) to the terminal ends of the DNA strands to target specific cell-surface markers. We will systematically vary ligand density and spacer length via DNA sequence design. These SNA constructs will be loaded with distinct payloads: (1) fluorophore-tagged DNA barcodes for multiplexed flow cytometry and imaging, and (2) siRNA or mRNA for genetic modulation. Targeting will be validated in vitro using co-cultures of primary human immune cells and in vivo using murine tumor models, analyzed by high-parameter spectral flow cytometry and in situ hybridization.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable platform for single-cell-subset-specific nanoparticle targeting, moving beyond bulk tissue or cell-type delivery. It will provide a new tool to map and manipulate cell-cell interactions within the TME with unprecedented precision, directly addressing a key question in the '33 Unresolved Questions' regarding controlling nanoparticle interactions with biological systems. The resulting structure-property relationships will inform the rational design of next-generation immunotherapeutics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the programmable, polyvalent interaction principles of SNAs—typically used for intracellular gene regulation—to solve the extracellular targeting problem of distinguishing between immune cell subsets based on subtle differences in surface marker combinations, a task beyond the capability of conventional antibody-conjugated particles.

proposals.title: Spherical Nucleic Acid (SNA) Vaccine Platform for Prophylactic Immunization Against Glioblastoma Multiforme in High-Risk Populations
proposals.motivation: Glioblastoma multiforme (GBM) remains a universally lethal cancer with a dismal prognosis, and there are no effective preventive strategies for individuals at high genetic or familial risk. Prophylactic cancer vaccination is a promising but underexplored frontier, critically hindered by the need for platforms that can safely and potently generate durable, tumor-specific immune memory. Pre-2026, Chad Mirkin's lab has pioneered Spherical Nucleic Acids (SNAs)—nanoparticles with dense, radially oriented nucleic acid shells—as a uniquely effective platform for therapeutic immunotherapy, demonstrating superior cellular uptake and immunostimulatory properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a prophylactic SNA-based vaccine, co-delivering GBM-associated neoantigen peptides and tailored adjuvant sequences, can be engineered to elicit a potent, durable, and tumor-specific cytotoxic T-cell memory response in high-risk models, thereby preventing or significantly delaying tumorigenesis.
proposals.method: We will design SNAs using a gold nanoparticle core functionalized with a dense shell of DNA encoding for validated GBM neoantigens (e.g., EGFRvIII, IDH1-R132H) and CpG oligonucleotide adjuvants. The structure-property relationships of these constructs, including oligonucleotide density, sequence design, and core size (informed by Mirkin's work on silica and crystalline templates), will be systematically optimized for dendritic cell uptake and antigen presentation. Vaccine efficacy will be evaluated in transgenic murine models of GBM predisposition, monitoring for tumor-specific T-cell memory formation, time to tumor onset, and overall survival compared to controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel nanomedicine paradigm for cancer prevention, translating the SNA platform from a therapeutic to a prophylactic modality. It will generate fundamental knowledge on the structural requirements of nanovaccines for inducing long-term immunological memory against solid tumors and provide a scalable, modular platform adaptable to other hereditary cancer syndromes.
proposals.novelty: While SNAs have been explored for therapeutic cancer vaccination, their application as a *prophylactic* vaccine is novel. The approach uniquely leverages the programmable architecture and known immunogenic properties of SNAs to address the unsolved challenge of pre-cancerous immunization, a direction highlighted as critical in recent pre-2026 nanoscience roadmaps.

proposals.title: Spherical Nucleic Acid (SNA) Architectures for Antigen-Specific Tolerance Induction in Neuroinflammatory Disease Models
proposals.motivation: Current treatments for autoimmune neuroinflammatory diseases like multiple sclerosis broadly suppress the immune system, leading to significant side effects and increased infection risk. There is a critical unmet need for antigen-specific tolerance strategies that selectively silence pathogenic T-cells without global immunosuppression. Nanoparticle-based platforms, particularly those leveraging precise structural control, offer a promising route to co-deliver autoantigens and tolerogenic signals directly to antigen-presenting cells.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that SNA constructs, with a dense, oriented shell of myelin-derived peptide antigens and co-conjugated tolerogenic oligonucleotides (e.g., immunosuppressive CpG or regulatory miRNA mimics), will reprogram antigen-presenting cells towards a tolerogenic phenotype, leading to the generation of antigen-specific regulatory T-cells and suppression of neuroinflammation in vivo.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize SNAs using a gold nanoparticle core functionalized with a dense shell of thiolated peptides from myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein (MOG35-55). Using established DNA conjugation chemistry, we will co-functionalize these structures with specific immunosuppressive TLR9 ligands (e.g., 'suppressive' CpG sequences) or STAT6-activating oligonucleotides. The structure-property relationships of these complexes—including ligand density, ratio, and spatial arrangement—will be systematically tuned and characterized. Their efficacy will be tested in vitro using bone marrow-derived dendritic cells and in vivo using the experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis (EAE) mouse model, monitoring disease progression, T-cell phenotypes, and cytokine profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of structurally defined, multi-functional SNA tolerogens for autoimmune disease. It will provide fundamental insights into how nanoscale architecture and ligand presentation dictate immune cell programming towards tolerance. The successful demonstration could yield a novel, potentially universal platform for antigen-specific immunotherapy of autoimmune disorders.
proposals.novelty: While SNAs are established for immunostimulation, their application as structurally precise tools for inducing antigen-specific tolerance is largely unexplored. The novelty lies in applying Mirkin's core design principles—high-density, oriented ligand presentation and programmable nucleic acid shells—to the inverse immunological problem of suppression rather than activation.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Spherical Nucleic Acid Architectures via Machine Learning on In Vivo Biodistribution and Efficacy Datasets
proposals.motivation: The rational design of nanoparticles for therapeutic delivery remains a major challenge due to the complex, non-linear relationship between nanoparticle structure (e.g., size, shape, surface chemistry) and in vivo performance (e.g., biodistribution, cellular uptake, efficacy). Traditional trial-and-error approaches are slow and costly. An inverse design framework, which starts from a desired biological outcome and works backward to an optimal structure, could dramatically accelerate the development of next-generation nanomedicines, particularly for established platforms like Spherical Nucleic Acids (SNAs).
proposals.hypothesis: Machine learning models trained on high-throughput in vivo data can learn the latent structure-property relationships of SNAs and other core-shell nanoparticles, enabling the inverse prediction of optimal structural parameters for a specified therapeutic outcome, such as tumor accumulation or gene silencing efficiency.
proposals.method: We will curate a large, structured dataset from published and in-house studies on SNA and silica nanoparticle in vivo performance. Input features will include core composition (Au, SiO2), size, shell density, oligonucleotide sequence/length, and targeting ligands. Output labels will be quantitative biodistribution and efficacy metrics. A conditional variational autoencoder (CVAE) will be trained on this dataset to learn a generative model of the design space. The model will then be used to solve the inverse problem: given a target organ accumulation profile or IC50 value, the CVAE decoder will propose candidate nanoparticle parameter sets, which will be validated through synthesis and in vivo testing.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, data-driven pipeline for the inverse design of complex nanocarriers, moving beyond heuristic design rules. It will produce a predictive model that maps biological outcomes to nanoparticle architectures, significantly shortening the development cycle for targeted therapies. The project will also generate a curated, public dataset to serve as a benchmark for the nanomedicine community.
proposals.novelty: While ML has been used to predict nanoparticle properties from structure, applying it to inversely design nanoparticles based directly on in vivo efficacy data is a paradigm shift. This approach uniquely integrates Mirkin's expertise in SNA synthesis and nanofabrication with machine learning to solve a core 'unresolved question' in nanoscience: how to optimally engineer interfaces for complex biological environments.

proposals.title: Spherical Nucleic Acid (SNA)-Functionalized Silica Nanoparticles with Tunable Surface Chemistry for Receptor-Mediated Transport Across the Blood-Brain Barrier
proposals.motivation: The blood-brain barrier (BBB) remains a formidable obstacle for delivering therapeutics to treat neurological diseases, from glioblastoma to neurodegenerative disorders. Pre-2026, nanoparticle strategies often rely on passive or single-ligand targeting, which suffer from low efficiency and specificity. A systematic, materials-centric approach to engineer nanoparticle-BBB interactions is critically needed.
proposals.hypothesis: That silica-core nanoparticles densely functionalized with SNAs presenting precisely tuned ratios of targeting ligands (e.g., transferrin, LDL receptor ligands) and stealth polymers (e.g., PEG) will enable efficient, receptor-mediated transcytosis across the BBB by multivalently engaging endogenous transport pathways while evading clearance.
proposals.method: We will synthesize monodisperse, fluorescent silica nanoparticles (20-80 nm) via established sol-gel methods. Using Mirkin's SNA conjugation techniques, we will functionalize these cores with dense shells of oligonucleotides. Through DNA hybridization, we will introduce controlled mixtures of ligands (e.g., transferrin, angiopep-2) and PEG chains. The structure and surface properties will be characterized by TEM, DLS, and UV-Vis. Transport efficiency will be quantified using an in vitro BBB model (hCMEC/D3 cell monolayer) and validated in vivo in mice via fluorescence and mass spectrometry.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a design rulebook linking SNA surface architecture (ligand identity, density, and ratio) to BBB translocation efficiency. It will produce a new class of modular, characterizable nanocarriers specifically engineered for brain delivery, moving beyond empirical formulations. The findings will directly address unresolved questions in nanoscience regarding nanoparticle-biological barrier interactions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the SNA platform—known for programmable interactions and cellular entry—to the BBB problem, enabling unprecedented control over multivalent ligand presentation on a silica core, a combination not explored for this application pre-2026.

proposals.title: Directed Evolution of Spherical Nucleic Acid (SNA) Architectures for Enhanced Cellular Uptake and Gene Regulation
proposals.motivation: The rational design of nanoparticles for specific biological functions, such as targeted delivery and gene regulation, is limited by the complexity of bio-nano interactions. As highlighted in pre-2026 reviews on unresolved questions in nanoscience, predicting how nanoparticle structure influences cellular behavior remains a major challenge. Directed evolution, a powerful biological optimization tool, offers a high-throughput, empirical solution to this design problem.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that iterative cycles of diversification and selection, applied to a library of SNA variants with tunable core composition and surface ligand density, will yield nanoparticle architectures with significantly enhanced cellular uptake efficiency and gene silencing activity compared to rationally designed counterparts.
proposals.method: We will establish a DNA-encoded SNA library where the nanoparticle core (e.g., varying silica size, gold composition) and surface oligonucleotide sequence/density are linked to a unique DNA barcode. Using a fluorescence-activated cell sorter (FACS), we will perform iterative selection cycles on cultured cells, isolating SNA populations with the highest uptake or target protein knockdown. High-throughput sequencing of the associated DNA barcodes will identify the winning structural features. These features will inform the synthesis of a refined, next-generation library for further evolution.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable platform for the evolution of functional nanomaterials, moving beyond trial-and-error design. It will produce new, non-intuitive structure-property relationships for SNAs and deliver evolved particles with benchmark performance for therapeutic delivery. The methodology will be a blueprint for optimizing other material properties, such as catalytic activity or optical response.
proposals.novelty: While directed evolution is standard in protein engineering, its application to the systematic optimization of synthetic nanoparticle structure, particularly complex architectures like SNAs, is novel. This approach directly addresses the 'unresolved questions' of predicting nano-bio interactions by letting cellular outcomes guide material design.

proposals.title: Programmable Co-Assembly of DNA Origami Nanostructures and Silica Precursors into Macroscopic, Hierarchically Ordered Materials
proposals.motivation: DNA origami enables the precise construction of complex nanostructures with programmable interactions, but scaling these discrete objects into continuous, macroscopic materials remains a major challenge. This limits their application in areas like photonics, catalysis, and tissue engineering, where bulk functional properties emerge from long-range order. Developing robust, scalable assembly methods is a critical unresolved question in nanoscience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by using DNA origami nanostructures as programmable, multivalent building blocks and co-assembling them with silica precursors, we can create macroscopic, self-supporting materials where the nanoscale architecture dictates the material's optical and mechanical properties.
proposals.method: We will design DNA origami tiles with 'sticky-end' interactions and specific surface chemistries to guide their hierarchical assembly into micron-scale frameworks. Inspired by previous work on spherical nucleic acids (SNAs) and silica encapsulation, we will infiltrate these frameworks with tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) under controlled sol-gel conditions. The DNA nanostructures will act as both a sacrificial template and a director for silica condensation, creating a continuous, reinforced silica composite. We will systematically vary the origami design (e.g., tile geometry, interaction valency) and silica processing parameters to control pore structure, density, and long-range order.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable platform for converting discrete DNA nanostructures into bulk functional materials, directly addressing the scale-up challenge. It will provide fundamental insights into how programmed nanoscale interactions translate to macroscopic material properties, such as photonic band structure and fracture toughness, bridging the fields of DNA nanotechnology and materials science.
proposals.novelty: While DNA-mediated crystallization of nanoparticles is established, the use of complex DNA origami as both structural and functional templates for sol-gel silica composites to create macroscopic monoliths is novel. This approach merges Mirkin's expertise in programmable interactions and silica materials with the structural precision of origami.

proposals.title: Silica-Encapsulated Multi-Component DNA-Programmed Superlattices: A Platform for Stabilized, Complex Functional Materials
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, DNA-programmable assembly enables the creation of sophisticated colloidal superlattices with multiple nanoparticle components, promising for advanced nanophotonics and catalysis. However, these complex, multi-component architectures are inherently fragile, limiting their study, processing, and application outside aqueous, near-equilibrium conditions. A robust stabilization method that preserves nanoscale order and composition is a critical unmet need to translate these designer structures into functional materials.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a conformal silica encapsulation method, previously used to stabilize single-component DNA-nanoparticle crystals, to complex multi-component superlattices will preserve their structural integrity, particle registry, and interfacial relationships, enabling their use in solid-state environments and detailed structure-property analysis.
proposals.method: We will design and assemble binary and ternary superlattices (e.g., CsPbBr3 perovskite nanocubes with Au spheres, or combinations of plasmonic and magnetic nanoparticles) using established DNA surface functionalization and hybridization techniques. The resulting crystalline hydrogels will be infiltrated with a silica precursor (tetraethyl orthosilicate, TEOS) under controlled pH and catalyst conditions to grow a conformal, mechanically robust silica matrix throughout the lattice. The stabilized solids will be characterized via in-situ small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS), electron microscopy, and optical spectroscopy to confirm preservation of structure and emergent properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general pathway to create solid-state, processable materials from the most complex DNA-assembled architectures, unlocking their potential for device integration. It will provide the first detailed study of the structure-optical property relationships in multi-component perovskite heterostructures with defined nanoscale interfaces, directly addressing unresolved questions in nanoscience regarding the characterization and application of complex nanomaterial systems.
proposals.novelty: While silica stabilization of simple DNA-NP crystals is known, its application to multi-component, multi-interface systems with distinct chemical and physical properties (e.g., halide perovskites) is unexplored and non-trivial, requiring precise control of the encapsulation chemistry to avoid disrupting delicate pre-formed interactions and compositions.

proposals.title: Single-Molecule Dual-Function Passivators for Halide Perovskites: Integrating Chemical Coordination and Field-Effect Passivation via Spherical Nucleic Acid Architectures
proposals.motivation: In halide perovskite photovoltaics and LEDs, achieving stable, high-performance devices requires simultaneous passivation of both ionic defects (e.g., Pb²⁺ vacancies, halide interstitials) and electronic defects (e.g., surface traps). Current state-of-the-art (pre-2026) relies on complex, multi-component treatments using distinct molecules for chemical coordination (e.g., Lewis bases) and field-effect passivation (e.g., ammonium salts), which complicates processing and can introduce interfacial instability. A unified molecular approach could simplify fabrication and enhance interfacial control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single, designer molecule, constructed as a spherical nucleic acid (SNA) with a dense, radially oriented shell of oligonucleotides, can provide both site-specific chemical coordination to perovskite surface ions and a permanent molecular dipole layer for field-effect passivation, thereby improving optoelectronic properties and stability.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize a library of core-shell SNAs where the inorganic core (e.g., silica nanoparticle or gold nanoparticle) provides a structural scaffold. The dense oligonucleotide shell will be functionalized with terminal groups selected for dual function: phosphonate or carboxylate groups for strong chemical coordination to under-coordinated Pb²⁺ sites, and quaternary ammonium or sulfonate groups at the oligonucleotide backbone to create a robust surface dipole for field-effect passivation. These SNAs will be assembled onto perovskite thin films (e.g., MAPbI₃, CsPbBr₃), and their binding, surface coverage, and dipole orientation will be characterized using XPS, FTIR, and Kelvin probe force microscopy. The optoelectronic impact will be assessed via photoluminescence lifetime, time-resolved microwave conductivity, and performance of prototype solar cells.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for multifunctional surface ligands in nanotechnology, moving beyond the 'one-molecule, one-function' paradigm. It will provide a concrete materials platform (SNA-based passivators) that simplifies perovskite device processing while potentially setting new benchmarks for interfacial stability and charge carrier lifetime. The findings will advance the fundamental understanding of how molecular architecture dictates complex surface interactions in hybrid materials.
proposals.novelty: While SNAs are known for programmable interactions and cellular delivery, their application as engineered, multifunctional surface ligands for inorganic semiconductors is unexplored. The novelty lies in the deliberate integration of two distinct passivation mechanisms—traditionally requiring separate molecular entities—into a single, structurally defined SNA construct, leveraging Mirkin's expertise in SNA design and nanosurface engineering.

proposals.title: Structure-Property Relationships in Diammonium Molecular Passivants: A Spherical Nucleic Acid (SNA)-Enabled Study for Enhanced Silicon Surface Field-Effect Passivation
proposals.motivation: Field-effect passivation of silicon surfaces, crucial for high-efficiency photovoltaics and electronic devices, is often achieved using organic ammonium salts. However, a systematic understanding of how molecular structure dictates electrostatic repulsion of minority carriers is lacking. This knowledge gap limits the rational design of optimal passivation layers, constraining device performance improvements.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the efficacy of diammonium molecules for field-effect passivation is a predictable function of their chain length and terminal group chemistry, which control their packing density, dipole orientation, and resultant surface potential on silicon.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of diammonium molecules with systematically varied alkane chain lengths and terminal groups (e.g., -NH3+, -PH3+, guanidinium). These molecules will be conjugated to gold nanoparticles to form spherical nucleic acids (SNAs), leveraging Mirkin's expertise to create highly ordered, crystalline monolayers on silica-coated silicon wafers. The structural order and packing will be characterized by grazing-incidence X-ray diffraction and spectroscopic ellipsometry, while field-effect passivation quality will be quantified by contactless photoconductance decay to measure effective carrier lifetime.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative structure-property relationship map for molecular field-effect passivants, directly linking synthetic chemistry to electronic performance. It will provide a design rule set for next-generation passivation layers and demonstrate the utility of SNA-based assembly for creating model, well-defined interfaces for materials science studies.
proposals.novelty: The use of SNA constructs to impose crystalline order on passivating molecules is a novel materials platform that transcends traditional self-assembled monolayers, enabling precise control over interfacial structure to decouple its effects from purely chemical ones. This approach is non-obvious within the passivation field, which typically focuses on bulk film properties.

proposals.title: Programmable Assembly of Nanoframe Lattices with Integrated Magnetic and Plasmonic Functionalities
proposals.motivation: Spherical nucleic acid (SNA) architectures and DNA-programmable assembly have enabled the construction of sophisticated colloidal crystals, but these materials are often limited to isotropic gold nanoparticle cores. Integrating anisotropic, hollow, or multifunctional building blocks, such as nanoframes, into these lattices remains a significant challenge. This work addresses a key gap in nanoscience by creating programmable, three-dimensional superlattices from nanoframes, which would provide unprecedented control over optical, magnetic, and catalytic properties through their complex, high-surface-area geometries and defined interstitial spaces.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by functionalizing the vertices of open-framework nanoframes (e.g., gold or palladium-based) with dense shells of oligonucleotides, we can program their assembly into crystalline superlattices, and that incorporating magnetic materials (e.g., iron oxide) or bimetallic compositions into the nanoframe structure will yield lattices with emergent and tunable collective properties not possible with solid nanoparticles.
proposals.method: We will synthesize monodisperse noble metal (Au, Pd) and magnetic (Fe3O4) nanoframes via galvanic replacement and seeded growth techniques, precisely controlling size and wall thickness. These nanoframes will be densely functionalized with thiolated DNA using established SNA conjugation protocols. Lattice assembly will be directed by complementary DNA sequences, with the structural outcome (crystal symmetry, lattice parameter) characterized by in-situ small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) and electron microscopy. The optical properties (plasmonic coupling) and magnetic responses of the bulk lattices will be measured via UV-Vis-NIR spectroscopy and superconducting quantum interference device (SQUID) magnetometry, respectively.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general platform for constructing macroscopic, ordered materials from complex nanoscale building blocks, moving beyond solid spheres. It will demonstrate how the integration of multiple material types (plasmonic, magnetic) within an open-framework lattice architecture can lead to new metamaterial properties, such as magneto-optical coupling or enhanced catalytic activity due to accessible high-surface-area sites. This directly addresses unresolved questions in nanoscience regarding the design of complex, hierarchical materials from the bottom-up.
proposals.novelty: While DNA-mediated assembly of nanoparticles is well-established, the programmable crystallization of high-surface-area, anisotropic nanoframes into three-dimensional superlattices has not been achieved. The deliberate combination of magnetic and plasmonic functionalities within the single building block and its resultant lattice creates a novel class of active, responsive metamaterials.

proposals.title: Dynamic Covalent DNA-Silica Coatings for Self-Healing Colloidal Crystals and Nanophotonic Materials
proposals.motivation: Spherical nucleic acid (SNA)-based colloidal crystals and superlattices are powerful platforms for programmable materials and nanophotonics, but their utility is limited by defect formation and mechanical fragility. Integrating self-healing concepts, well-established in bulk polymers, into nanoscale DNA-driven assembly remains a major challenge. This work addresses a key unresolved question in nanoscience: how to create robust, defect-tolerant nanostructured materials that can recover their designed optical properties after damage.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating dynamic covalent bonds (e.g., boronate esters, disulfides, or Diels-Alder adducts) into the DNA backbone or the silica coating of SNAs will create a reversible, stimuli-responsive interface that enables the autonomous repair of lattice defects and the recovery of long-range order and photonic properties.
proposals.method: We will synthesize SNAs with core-shell architectures, where the silica shell is functionalized with dynamic covalent moieties via silane chemistry. Parallel designs will feature DNA strands modified with dynamic linkers at their termini. Using Mirkin's established design rules, we will assemble these particles into crystalline lattices and characterize their structure via SAXS/TEM. We will then induce controlled mechanical or thermal damage and monitor lattice self-repair in situ using optical spectroscopy and microscopy, correlating healing kinetics with bond exchange rates and environmental triggers (pH, light, redox).
proposals.contribution: This will establish the first design principles for self-healing, DNA-programmable superlattices, directly extending the 'materials-by-design' paradigm to include dynamic, reparative function. It will yield a new class of resilient nanophotonic materials whose optical stop-bands and plasmonic couplings can recover from disruption, enhancing their viability in sensing and optical computing applications.
proposals.novelty: While self-healing polymers and static DNA crystals are known, the integration of dynamic covalent chemistry into the precise, programmable interface of SNAs to create self-repairing superlattices is a novel materials strategy. This moves beyond mere assembly to incorporate adaptive, life-like functionality into inorganic-organic nanomaterial architectures.

proposals.title: Programmable Colloidal Crystallization via Minimalist ssDNA and Peptide Linkers for Tailored Optical Properties
proposals.motivation: Spherical nucleic acid (SNA) architectures and DNA-programmable colloidal crystals rely on dense, double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) linkers, which introduce significant interstitial organic material. This interstitial matrix can obscure nanoparticle interfaces, dampen plasmonic and excitonic coupling, and complicate the analysis of structure-property relationships in photonic and optoelectronic materials. Reducing this complexity is critical for creating cleaner, more defined superlattices with enhanced and predictable optical responses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that replacing conventional dsDNA duplexes with rationally designed, minimal single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) or short peptide linkers will yield structurally robust, low-interstitial-fraction superlattices with superior optical properties, including sharper plasmonic resonances and enhanced charge/energy transfer, due to reduced dielectric screening and shorter interparticle distances.
proposals.method: We will synthesize gold and halide perovskite nanocrystals functionalized with thiolated ssDNA sequences or cysteine-terminated peptides. Lattice symmetry and interparticle spacing will be programmed by varying the length (5-15 bases/amino acids) and sequence of these minimalist linkers. Crystallization will be driven by thermal annealing or solvent evaporation. Structures will be characterized by in-situ SAXS and cryo-EM. Optical properties (extinction, photoluminescence, ultrafast kinetics) will be correlated with lattice parameters and linker composition, guided by finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) simulations.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design paradigm for colloidal crystals by minimizing the organic linker phase, directly addressing a key challenge in nanoscience. It will provide fundamental insights into how ultra-short, flexible linkers control crystallization and will deliver material systems with optimized optical properties for potential applications in nanophotonics and sensing.
proposals.novelty: While DNA and peptides are established as linkers, the systematic use of *minimal* ssDNA or short peptides as the *sole* programmable bonding element for 3D crystallization, explicitly to reduce interstitial material and enhance optical coupling, is a significant departure from the dense dsDNA brush layer paradigm central to Mirkin's prior work.

proposals.title: Programming Mammalian Cellular Responses via Engineered Receptors for Patterned DNA Signal Transduction
proposals.motivation: Current synthetic biology relies heavily on diffusible chemical or optogenetic signals, which lack the spatial precision and multiplexing potential of direct nucleic acid recognition. A system where cells directly interpret complex, non-diffusible DNA patterns as primary inputs would enable unprecedented control in tissue engineering, high-density biosensing, and interfacing with DNA-based materials and devices. This addresses a core challenge in creating smart, responsive cellular systems that can interact with engineered nanoscale environments.
proposals.hypothesis: By engineering chimeric antigen receptors (CARs) or synthetic Notch (synNotch) receptors with extracellular DNA-binding domains (e.g., zinc fingers or TALEs), mammalian cells can be programmed to detect specific surface-immobilized oligonucleotide patterns, triggering precise, user-defined transcriptional or metabolic outputs.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize DNA-binding domains (DBDs) targeting orthogonal 12-18 bp sequences. These DBDs will be fused to transmembrane and intracellular signaling domains (e.g., CD3ζ for CARs, Gal4-VP64 for synNotch) via flexible linkers. Receptor constructs will be expressed in HEK-293T and Jurkat cells. Patterned DNA signals will be fabricated on silica and gold substrates using Dip-Pen Nanolithography (DPN), a core Mirkin group expertise, creating spatially defined 'DNA antigen' landscapes. Cellular activation will be quantified via fluorescence (GFP reporter) and secreted factors (ELISA) in response to contact with specific patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational platform for 'DNA pattern recognition' by engineered cells, creating a new interface between synthetic biology and scanning probe nanofabrication. It will demonstrate a novel signaling paradigm where nanoscale spatial organization of nucleic acids directly dictates cellular behavior, contributing to the fields of bio-interfacial materials and programmable cellular therapeutics.
proposals.novelty: While DNA is used for cell-surface engineering and logic gates, using immobilized, patterned DNA oligonucleotides as the primary ligand for engineered receptor signaling is unexplored. This merges the precision of DPN-based nanofabrication with the programmability of synthetic receptors to create a direct material-to-cell communication channel.

proposals.title: Programmable Chemical Lithography on DNA Hydrogels via CRISPR-dCas9-Enzyme Fusions
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the field of DNA nanotechnology excels at creating static nanostructures, but lacks robust, programmable methods for writing dynamic chemical patterns onto DNA substrates with high spatial resolution. This limits the creation of smart, responsive materials and multiplexed biosensing platforms. Integrating the programmability of CRISPR with the structural control of DNA materials could bridge this gap, enabling a new class of reconfigurable biointerfaces.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dCas9, fused to enzymatic effectors (e.g., methyltransferases, transglutaminases, or polymerases) and guided by specific sgRNAs, can be used to write precise, user-defined chemical modifications onto DNA strands immobilized within a polyacrylamide-DNA copolymer hydrogel, creating programmable chemical patterns.
proposals.method: We will synthesize polyacrylamide hydrogels with embedded, addressable single-stranded DNA arrays designed with orthogonal protospacer adjacent motifs (PAMs). Purified dCas9 fusions with enzymes like M.TaqI methyltransferase will be complexed with sgRNAs and introduced to the hydrogel. The sgRNA will direct the fusion to specific DNA addresses, where the enzyme will catalyze a local chemical modification (e.g., methylation, biotinylation, or primer extension). Patterns will be visualized via fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) with modified nucleotide probes or antibody staining, and characterized using atomic force microscopy and fluorescence microscopy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general platform for 'chemical lithography' on biocompatible substrates, merging the fields of CRISPR-based targeting and synthetic DNA materials. It will contribute a new tool for creating spatially encoded hydrogels for cell culture, multiplexed diagnostic arrays, and the fundamental study of multivalent ligand-receptor interactions. It directly addresses unresolved questions in nanoscience regarding the programmable assembly of complex, functional interfaces.
proposals.novelty: While dCas9 has been used for genomic imaging and regulation, its application as a direct, programmable writer of chemical patterns on synthetic DNA materials is unexplored. The novelty lies in leveraging Mirkin's expertise in DNA-mediated materials assembly and multivalent interactions to create a non-genomic, materials-focused application of CRISPR technology.

proposals.title: Quantifying DNA Surface Patterning Fidelity via Click-Chemistry Functionalized DNA-PAINT Super-Resolution Microscopy
proposals.motivation: Precise spatial patterning of oligonucleotides on surfaces is fundamental for constructing advanced DNA-based materials and diagnostic devices, such as Spherical Nucleic Acids (SNAs) and multiplexed biosensors. However, pre-2026 characterization methods lack the resolution to directly visualize and quantify the nanoscale density and distribution of immobilized DNA strands, creating a critical gap between designed surface chemistry and realized functional properties. This limits the rational design of interfaces for controlled molecular interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating bioorthogonal click-chemistry for DNA immobilization with DNA-PAINT super-resolution microscopy will enable quantitative, nanoscale mapping of strand density and spatial order, revealing that current patterning methods produce significant heterogeneity that correlates with functional performance in hybridization assays.
proposals.method: We will functionalize silica and gold substrates with azide or DBCO groups using silane or thiol chemistry, respectively. DNA strands with complementary cyclooctyne or azide modifiers will be conjugated via copper-free click reactions. DNA-PAINT imaging will be performed using transient binding of dye-labeled imager strands to the immobilized docking sequences. Custom analysis algorithms, based on single-molecule localization and point-pattern analysis (e.g., Ripley's K-function), will be developed to calculate local strand density, nearest-neighbor distances, and spatial clustering from the super-resolved maps.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, quantitative method to correlate DNA surface patterning chemistry with nanoscale structural outcomes, moving beyond ensemble averages. It will provide design rules for optimizing DNA-functionalized materials and interfaces, directly impacting fields from nanoparticle superlattice engineering to single-molecule biosensing by linking structure to interaction properties.
proposals.novelty: While DNA-PAINT is used for biological imaging, its adaptation as a metrology tool for synthetic DNA surface patterns, specifically coupled with robust click-chemistry immobilization, is novel. This approach merges Mirkin's expertise in DNA-based materials and nanofabrication with super-resolution microscopy to solve a core materials characterization problem.

proposals.title: Spatially Addressable Spherical Nucleic Acid (SNA) Arrays for Subcellular Confinement and Manipulation of Signaling Domains
proposals.motivation: Understanding how spatial organization of signaling molecules at the subcellular scale dictates cell fate and function remains a major challenge in cell biology. Pre-2026 tools, like micropatterning, lack the resolution to isolate nanoscale signaling domains such as lipid rafts or receptor clusters. This project aims to bridge this gap by creating a platform to physically and chemically confine signaling events with nanoscale precision, enabling direct interrogation of spatial control in cellular decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that creating lithographically defined, chemically functionalized patterns smaller than a single cell will allow for the physical isolation and selective activation of specific subcellular signaling domains, and that the spatial arrangement of these patterns will directly dictate downstream signaling outcomes.
proposals.method: We will leverage dip-pen nanolithography (DPN) and polymer pen lithography (PPL) to fabricate sub-micron arrays of adhesive and non-adhesive regions on silica and gold substrates. The patterns will be functionalized with specific signaling ligands (e.g., epidermal growth factor) conjugated to spherical nucleic acid (SNA) architectures to enhance stability and multivalent presentation. High-resolution live-cell imaging (TIRF, super-resolution microscopy) will be used to monitor protein recruitment and signaling dynamics (e.g., ERK activation) within these confined areas, correlating pattern geometry with cellular response.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new materials-based toolkit for the nanoscale spatial manipulation of cellular signaling, moving beyond whole-cell stimulation to domain-specific interrogation. It will provide fundamental insights into how the geometry and density of signaling inputs are interpreted by the cell, with implications for designing advanced biomaterials and understanding signaling dysregulation in disease.
proposals.novelty: While cell patterning is established, creating patterns smaller than a cell to isolate *intracellular* signaling compartments is a significant scaling challenge. The integration of high-resolution nanolithography with the programmable bio-interfacial properties of SNAs for subcellular confinement represents a novel, materials-driven approach to dissect spatial signaling.

ideas.title: Facet-Specific Destabilization via Selective Capping Agent Adsorption
ideas.description: This idea adapts the concept of competitive inhibition from enzymology to nanoparticle synthesis. In enzymes, competitive inhibitors are designed to bind specifically to the active site, blocking its function. Analogously, we propose designing capping agents that are not passive stabilizers but active, selective destabilizers. These agents would be engineered with head groups that have high affinity for the atomic arrangement and coordination of a *specific* target facet (e.g., {100} over {111}). Upon binding, the agent would not passivate the surface but would instead introduce destabilizing interactions—such as localized lattice strain, charge repulsion, or preferential solvation—that make that facet a 'hot spot' for etching or inhibit its growth during synthesis. This would invert the traditional role of capping agents from facet stabilizers to facet-specific destabilizing agents, enabling novel morphologies by selectively removing certain facets.

ideas.title: Sequence-Defined Foldamers as Programmable Shape-Complementary Linkers for Self-Assembly
ideas.description: This approach uses synthetic, sequence-defined foldamers (specifically, N-substituted glycine peptoids) as linkers. The monomer sequence is designed to direct folding into stable secondary structures with predictable three-dimensional displays of interaction motifs. Complementary pairs of these folded structures are engineered to bind via shape-selective, multivalent non-covalent interactions orthogonal to biological systems. This provides a cheaper, more stable, and chemically diverse platform for programmable nanoscale self-assembly, directly addressing the need for a predictable pairing mechanism that the original idea lacked.

ideas.title: Kinetically-Controlled Facet Manipulation via Catalytic Capping Agents
ideas.description: This research moves beyond equilibrium stabilization to exploit the kinetic role of capping agents for active morphological control. We design and synthesize molecular or polymeric capping agents that selectively bind to targeted crystal facets and catalyze their growth or dissolution via tailored surface reactions. The approach integrates computational screening for agents that induce favorable surface reconstructions or reaction pathways with real-time, in situ microscopy and spectroscopy to validate the kinetic mechanism. The outcome is a predictive framework for using 'catalytic cappers' to sculpt nanoparticle morphologies that are kinetically trapped and distinct from thermodynamic shapes.

ideas.title: Adapting Protein Docking Algorithms for Patchy Particle Crystal Design
ideas.description: This idea applies computational models for anisotropic patchy particles to predict and design DNA-mediated polyhedral crystal assembly by drawing an analogy from computational structural biology. The analogous problem is predicting how two proteins with specific, complementary surface patches dock together, which is solved using algorithms like RosettaDock that employ geometric hashing, FFT-based search, and physics/statistical scoring functions. The adapted solution is to use the core sampling and scoring framework of protein docking algorithms, but apply it to the assembly of patchy colloidal particles. The 'patches' are defined by the spatial arrangement of DNA strands on the particle surface. The algorithm would systematically search for low-energy, stable configurations of multiple patchy particles that match a target polyhedral crystal lattice, scoring each configuration based on the thermodynamics of DNA bond formation and patch alignment, thereby enabling the inverse design of particle surface functionalization for specific crystal structures.

ideas.title: Tensegrity-Guided Lithographic Assembly of Reconfigurable Colloidal Crystals
ideas.description: This research direction combines photolithographic substrate patterning with colloidal tensegrity structures. Precisely patterned adhesive patches on a substrate guide the initial placement and orientation of rigid colloidal particles, defining a seed crystal lattice. These particles are then interconnected by a network of programmable, tensile DNA linkers that introduce a tunable internal stress field, following tensegrity principles. The synergy allows the lithographic template to dictate the starting crystal symmetry and orientation, while the DNA tensegrity network enables dynamic, stimuli-responsive reconfiguration of the entire crystal lattice—such as switching between cubic and tetragonal phases—without losing registry with the underlying substrate, enabling active photonic or plasmonic metasurfaces.

ideas.title: Crystallographic Facet Control in Alloy Nanoparticles via Biomimetic Mineralization Principles
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept of applying a guided regulation framework to multi-metallic nanoparticles by drawing an analogy from biomineralization. In nature, organisms use organic templates to exert exquisite control over inorganic crystal growth, dictating specific facets and morphologies. The adapted research idea proposes to mimic this solution by designing synthetic molecular or polymeric regulators that selectively adsorb to target crystallographic planes or atomic sites on a growing multi-metallic nanoparticle. Integrating these biomimetic regulators into the guided synthesis framework would allow for the kinetic and thermodynamic steering of alloy composition and shell deposition, ultimately enabling the programmable control of exposed facets in complex nanoparticle systems for tailored catalytic or electronic properties.

ideas.title: Spatially Addressable DNA Linkers for Programmable Domain-Specific Reconfiguration of Colloidal Crystals
ideas.description: This redesigned system integrates multiple orthogonal sets of stimuli-responsive DNA linkers, each designed to react to a distinct, localized trigger such as a specific wavelength of light or a unique chemical effector. This allows for the selective and independent reconfiguration of pre-defined domains within a single colloidal crystal lattice. Consequently, complex, heterogeneous photonic architectures—such as patterned bandgaps, waveguides, or logic gates—can be dynamically written and erased within the material. The approach moves beyond bulk transformation to enable true spatiotemporal programming of crystal structure and function.

ideas.title: Apply architectural tensegrity principles to DNA-nanoparticle interface design
ideas.description: This idea adapts architectural tensegrity principles to design DNA-nanoparticle assemblies. In tensegrity structures, stability emerges from balanced tension-compression networks rather than rigid connections. Here, computational force-balance algorithms model DNA strands as tensile 'cables' and nanoparticles as compressive 'struts,' determining minimal, optimal DNA interface placements on polyhedral facets. This approach creates self-assembling structures maintained by balanced DNA tension, reducing material use while enhancing mechanical stability compared to full facet functionalization, enabling more efficient and robust nanoscale construction.

ideas.title: Closed-loop computer vision feedback for adaptive DNA-programmed colloidal crystals
ideas.description: This research direction integrates stimuli-responsive DNA linkers with automated computer vision analysis to create an adaptive, feedback-controlled materials system. A colloidal crystal with dynamic DNA linkers is imaged in situ (e.g., via liquid-phase TEM or optical microscopy) during a triggered transformation. Computer vision algorithms continuously quantify facet morphology, lattice parameters, and defect evolution in real time. This quantitative morphological data is then fed back into a control system that decides the nature, intensity, or duration of the next applied stimulus (e.g., light, temperature, chemical), enabling the crystal to be intelligently guided toward a target structure or to recover from undesired states autonomously.

ideas.title: Adapting Protein Folding Energy Landscapes to Map Sub-Nanometer Cluster Stability
ideas.description: This idea investigates the applicability of facet regulation principles to sub-nanometer clusters where facets are not well-defined by drawing an analogy from computational biochemistry. In protein folding, the solution for studying short peptides without fixed structures involves mapping their conformational energy landscapes to find stable motifs. Adapted to nanocatalysis, the research will computationally map the free energy landscape of ultra-small metal clusters (e.g., Au, Pt, Pd with <20 atoms) using DFT and ab initio MD. The goal is to identify prevalent 'stability basins' (specific, recurring atomic configurations) and test if adsorbates, supports, or synthetic conditions can selectively stabilize certain basins, thereby controlling cluster properties in a manner analogous to facet regulation in larger nanoparticles.

ideas.title: Rigid DNA Origami Connectors with Localized Compliance for Robust Shape Complementarity
ideas.description: This approach designs rigid, angled DNA origami connectors that enforce specific orientations between components, but critically integrates small, localized flexible elements at the binding interfaces. These compliance elements, such as short ssDNA stretches or two-helix bundle hinges, act as shock absorbers for thermal motion and fabrication tolerances, preventing steric clashes. The core rigidity of the linker thus guides assembly into the desired shape, while the localized flexibility guarantees high-yield binding even with imperfect parts. This hybrid design combines the precision of rigid spacers with the robustness of flexible ones.

ideas.title: Adapt Tensegrity Colloidal Crystals via Pre-stressed Cable-Net Structures
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept of using architectural tensegrity principles for colloidal crystals by drawing an analogy from structural engineering's use of pre-stressed cable-net structures. The analogous solution involves actively tensioning a network of cables against compressive elements to define a stable, rigid form. Adapted to the research context, this involves incorporating DNA linkers that can be triggered (e.g., via a temperature shift or a specific chemical stimulus) to contract or shorten after initial assembly of the rigid colloidal shapes. This active pre-tensioning step puts the DNA 'cables' under precise tensile stress, balancing the compression in the rigid colloids to programmably lock the assembly into a desired, mechanically stable tensegrity lattice, enabling the creation of colloidal crystals with tunable stiffness and response to external forces.

ideas.title: Structure-Activity Mapping via Quantitative Structure-Property Relationships (QSPR) for Catalyst Facets
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the Quantitative Structure-Property Relationship (QSPR) approach from computational drug discovery to the field of catalyst design. In drug discovery, QSAR models correlate molecular descriptors with biological activity to predict and design new drugs. Here, we propose developing a Catalytic Facet-QSPR (CF-QSPR) model. First, a database of known catalyst materials with characterized dominant facets and measured catalytic performance data (e.g., turnover frequency, selectivity for a target reaction) is compiled. For each facet, a set of computable descriptors—such as generalized coordination number, average surface energy, electronic structure features from DFT (like d-band center or Bader charges), and adsorption energies for key intermediate species—is calculated. Machine learning or multivariate statistical analysis is then used to establish a correlation model between these facet descriptors and the catalytic performance metrics. Once trained and validated, this model can be applied to the 'link predicted facets directly to catalytic performance' concept: for a new, computationally predicted or engineered facet structure, its descriptors are calculated and fed into the CF-QSPR model to directly output a predicted performance score, thereby closing the loop between facet prediction and targeted catalytic design.

ideas.title: Inverse Design of Synthesis Pathways for Macroscopic Single-Crystalline Space-Tiled Architectures
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the goal of macroscopic, defect-free crystalline domains with the inverse design of synthesis conditions into a unified computational-experimental framework. The core is an inverse model that takes a user-specified target—defined by both a precise facet distribution (e.g., for catalytic activity) and a minimum single-crystalline domain size—and outputs a tailored synthesis pathway optimized to achieve both criteria simultaneously. This moves beyond simply predicting conditions for a facet pattern or empirically improving crystal size, as it explicitly solves for the thermodynamic and kinetic parameters that suppress polycrystalline growth and defects while directing the desired surface tiling. The outcome is a predictive platform for the on-demand fabrication of large-scale, single-crystalline metamaterials with engineered surface properties.

ideas.title: Atomic Motif Descriptors for Catalytic Regulation in Sub-Nanometer Clusters
ideas.description: This research systematically investigates the structural and electronic principles that govern the properties of ultra-small metal clusters (sub-1 nm), moving beyond the ill-defined concept of facets. By synthesizing precise clusters of controlled nuclearity and composition, and characterizing them with advanced spectroscopy and theory, the work aims to identify key atomic-scale motifs—such as specific coordination sites and electronic states—that serve as the fundamental descriptors for catalytic activity and selectivity. The outcome will be a new, more rigorous framework for understanding and designing catalysts at the ultimate size limit, where every atom's position matters.

ideas.title: Continuous-Flow Reticular Synthesis of Programmable Nanocrystal Superlattices
ideas.description: This research direction integrates reticular chemistry design principles directly into a continuous-flow synthesis system. A computational pre-calculation module, based on MOF design concepts, determines optimal nanocrystal shapes, surface chemistries, and linker molecules for target superlattice geometries. This digital blueprint then dynamically guides a multi-stage continuous flow reactor, where nanocrystal formation, surface functionalization, and guided tessellation occur in successive, optimized zones. The result is a scalable manufacturing platform that can produce bulk quantities of metamaterials with pre-programmed optical, catalytic, or mechanical properties derived from their designed nanocrystal superlattice structure.

ideas.title: Active Learning for Entropic Assembly of Mismatched Colloidal Polyhedra
ideas.description: This research integrates an active learning loop directly into the experimental exploration of entropically driven assembly. A machine learning model, trained on initial experiments where deliberately imperfect polyhedral colloids are combined with depletants, proposes the next most informative synthesis condition—such as a specific shape asymmetry or depletant concentration—to test the hypothesis of space-filling order without perfect complementarity. This closed-loop approach efficiently navigates the high-dimensional parameter space of shape mismatch and interaction strength, aiming to discover new thermodynamic phases and assembly rules that would be impractical to find through random or grid-based screening. The outcome is both a set of novel, entropy-stabilized colloidal materials and a predictive model for their design.

ideas.title: Dynamic Optical Template Guidance for Defect-Free Colloidal Crystallization
ideas.description: This approach uses real-time holographic optical tweezers to project dynamic, reconfigurable potential energy landscapes that guide colloidal crystal assembly. An imaging system provides live feedback on particle positions, enabling the optical template to adaptively adjust its lattice parameters and trap stiffness to correct misalignments and anneal defects as they form. This closed-loop control overcomes the rigidity of physical templates, allowing for the assembly of large, single-grain colloidal crystals with minimal defects. The system can also programmatically introduce and control desired defect geometries for functional metamaterials.

ideas.title: Closed-loop inverse design and hybrid fabrication of robust, integrable colloidal crystal metamaterial devices
ideas.description: This research creates a system to overcome the fabrication-to-application gap in colloidal metamaterials by tightly coupling design, synthesis, and integration. A machine learning model, trained on high-throughput experimental data from an expanded colloidal crystal library, learns the complex relationships between synthesis parameters, final structure, optical properties, and structural robustness. This model is used within an inverse design loop to generate blueprints for metamaterial structures that meet specific photonic bandgap targets while also being inherently stable and compatible with downstream encapsulation and device integration processes. The output is not just a material with desired properties, but a validated fabrication protocol for a functional, resilient optical component ready for testing in a target application.

ideas.title: Probabilistic Generative Inverse Design for Nanocrystal Faceting
ideas.description: This system addresses the non-uniqueness of the inverse problem by learning a generative model of synthesis conditions conditioned on a target facet distribution. Instead of predicting a single set of parameters, it outputs a diverse set of plausible synthesis recipes or a probability distribution over parameter space, capturing the many-to-one nature of the mapping. This allows users to explore the design space, assess the robustness and likelihood of proposed conditions, and select optimal pathways based on secondary constraints like cost or scalability. The approach fundamentally acknowledges and works with the inherent uncertainty of the inverse design challenge.

ideas.title: Quantum-Constrained Shape Complementarity for Predictive Crystal Assembly
ideas.description: This approach designs novel bulk crystals by enforcing a dual constraint during computational assembly: geometric shape complementarity and quantum mechanical stability. Proposed atomic or molecular building blocks are first evaluated for their intrinsic electronic viability and bonding preferences using first-principles calculations. A generative search algorithm then explores packing configurations that satisfy both efficient, lock-and-key style fitting and the fundamental rules of chemical bonding. This method aims to discover synthesizable, metastable materials with tailored properties, where the shape guide directs the search space but quantum mechanics provides the final selection filter.

ideas.title: Inverse Design of Colloidal Metamaterials via Topology Optimization
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the inverse design methodology of topology optimization from structural engineering to the design of metamaterials. The analogous problem—finding an optimal material layout for a target mechanical property—is solved computationally by defining a design domain, simulating performance, and using gradient-based optimization to iteratively update the material distribution. Adapted to the original idea, this approach would use the expanded library of space-filling colloidal crystals as a rich design space. A computational framework would be developed to inversely design the precise arrangement of colloidal particles within a unit cell. By coupling finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) or finite element method (FEM) simulations for optical or acoustic wave propagation with an optimization algorithm, the system would automatically discover novel, non-intuitive colloidal crystal geometries that yield targeted photonic or phononic bandgaps, moving beyond trial-and-error towards a goal-oriented design paradigm for metamaterials.

ideas.title: Epitaxial Colloidal Crystal Growth Using Lithographic Seed Patterns
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core principle of epitaxial growth from Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) for semiconductor thin films to the directed assembly of colloidal crystals. In MBE, the atomic lattice of the substrate templates the orientation of the growing film. Here, a photolithographically patterned substrate is designed not just as a generic guide, but as an 'epitaxial seed layer' for mesoscale particles. The template will feature a periodic array of topographic or chemical features (e.g., posts, binding sites, or etched pits) whose symmetry and periodicity are carefully matched to a specific crystallographic plane of the target colloidal crystal (e.g., (100) of an FCC lattice). During colloidal assembly, these pre-defined sites will act as preferential nucleation points, dictating the registry and orientation of the first particle layer. Subsequent layers will then grow in a controlled, epitaxial manner from this seeded template, resulting in a large-area, single-orientation colloidal crystal with dramatically reduced grain boundaries, analogous to a single-crystal semiconductor film grown by MBE.

ideas.title: Evolutionary Algorithm for Aperiodic Polyhedral Aggregate Design
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from computational biology's protein design field, where evolutionary algorithms optimize functional properties without being constrained by natural symmetry or periodicity. The analogous solution involves using genetic algorithms to explore vast conformational spaces by defining a structural 'genome,' applying a property-based fitness function, and iteratively selecting and mutating candidates. Adapted to materials science, this approach involves creating aperiodic, space-filling aggregates of polyhedra by defining connection rules and local constraints as an evolvable code. An evolutionary algorithm will then generate populations of aggregate structures, simulate their target properties (e.g., mechanical strength via finite element analysis, conductivity via network models), and use this performance as a fitness score to drive selection and variation over many generations. This method systematically bypasses crystal symmetry to discover novel, high-performing disordered or quasi-ordered architectures that optimize material properties.

ideas.title: Mapping the Dynamic Emergence of Facets from Fluxional Ultra-Small Clusters
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the synthesis of ultra-small, sub-nanometer metal clusters where the concept of a static facet does not initially exist. By applying dynamic evolution modeling to these fluxional, non-crystalline precursors, the work will track how transient atomic arrangements and ligand environments coalesce into the first stable crystallographic planes. This reveals the fundamental nucleation pathway of facets themselves, bridging the gap between amorphous clusters and faceted nanocrystals. The synergy provides a bottom-up, atomistic movie of facet birth, which is inaccessible by studying either defined facets or static clusters alone.

ideas.title: Predicting emergent functional properties of entropically assembled, shape-mismatched frameworks
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the functional materials that emerge from the entropy-driven assembly of polyhedral building blocks without perfect shape complementarity. The core innovation is to use machine learning to establish a direct predictive link between the statistical descriptors of the resulting, often disordered, interfacial facets (e.g., facet type distribution, local curvature, pore geometry) and the ensemble's macroscopic functional properties, such as gas adsorption energy or catalytic activity. This predictive model then guides the inverse design of new, deliberately mismatched building blocks intended to self-assemble into aggregates whose facet landscape optimizes a target function. Thus, the synergy enables the rational design of functional materials where the desired property is an emergent consequence of engineered entropic disorder, rather than precise geometric templating.

ideas.title: Continuous Flow Nanoparticle Synthesis via Metabolic Pathway Control Principles
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from industrial biotechnology's continuous fermentation processes, where metabolic pathway flux is dynamically regulated for consistent product synthesis, to the challenge of scaling nanoparticle production from batch to continuous flow. The analogous field is Industrial Biotechnology/Metabolic Engineering, where the solution involves using real-time biosensor data (e.g., pH, dissolved oxygen) in feedback loops to adjust nutrient feeds and maintain optimal metabolic pathway activity in a continuous bioreactor. The adapted research idea applies this principle by implementing an in-line analytical sensor suite (e.g., UV-Vis, DLS, Raman spectroscopy) within a continuous flow nanoparticle synthesis platform. The real-time data on nanoparticle characteristics feeds into a guided regulation framework that dynamically adjusts precursor injection rates, flow velocities, and zoned reactor temperatures. This creates a self-optimizing, closed-loop continuous production system that maintains precise control over nanoparticle properties, overcoming the drift and inconsistency typical of open-loop continuous processes.

ideas.title: Epitaxial Seeding and Slow Annealing for Micron-Scale Single-Crystalline Space-Tiled Architectures
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principle of epitaxial seeding and controlled growth from semiconductor thin-film technology to the synthesis of space-tiled crystalline architectures. In semiconductors, large single-crystalline domains are achieved by growing films on lattice-matched single-crystal substrates under slow, kinetically controlled conditions. The adapted research idea proposes designing a specialized template layer that seeds the desired tiling pattern. By then executing the synthesis of the space-tiled material via a slow, diffusion-limited process (e.g., controlled vapor deposition or solvent evaporation), the system is guided to propagate a single crystallographic orientation from the seed. This method aims to suppress random nucleation and polycrystalline grain boundaries, thereby achieving defect-free, single-crystalline domains of the complex architecture spanning many microns.

ideas.title: Inverse Design of Crystal Facets via Optimal Control Theory
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to the inverse trajectory design problem from aerospace engineering to solve the inverse design problem for crystal facet synthesis. In the analogous field, optimal control theory is used to find the necessary control inputs (like thrust profiles) to hit a desired endpoint, given a forward model of the system dynamics. Here, we frame crystal growth as a dynamical system where synthesis conditions (precursor ratios, temperature ramps, etc.) are the controls, and the evolving crystal surface is the state. By applying numerical optimal control methods (e.g., direct collocation) to a calibrated forward growth model, we can computationally solve for the precise synthesis 'trajectory' required to achieve any user-specified target distribution of crystal facets, inverting the forward design paradigm.

The key synergy is using the model's predictions as a feedback signal to guide and optimize the synthesis or processing conditions for the next batch of materials, aiming to produce specific, desired facet distributions. This creates an autonomous, closed-loop experimental framework that iteratively improves material performance by directly linking microstructural image data to target properties through an active learning cycle.

ideas.title: Entropic Assembly of Mismatched Shapes via Depletion-Induced Jamming
ideas.description: This idea explores if entropic or depletion forces can drive space-filling order from deliberately mismatched polyhedral shapes. The analogy is drawn from granular and soft matter physics, where mixtures of non-complementary shapes form rigid, jammed packings under cyclic driving forces like vibration. The adapted solution is to subject a colloidal suspension of mismatched polyhedral particles and depletants to an oscillatory global drive, such as pressure or temperature cycles. This process uses depletion attraction to promote assembly while the cyclic energy input prevents kinetic arrest in flawed local minima, enabling the system to jostle into a collectively jammed, space-filling ordered state where the global constraint of density, not local shape fit, dictates the final structure.

ideas.title: 3D Tomographic Reconstruction and Geometric Analysis for True Facet Morphology Quantification
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the limitations of 2D image analysis by first acquiring a 3D structural dataset, typically via electron tomography or multi-angle SEM imaging. A computational pipeline then reconstructs a volumetric model, segments the nanoparticle surface, and identifies discrete facets through surface normal clustering and curvature analysis. The system outputs robust, projection-artifact-free metrics, including true facet area distributions, crystallographic orientation maps (when combined with diffraction data), and statistical descriptors of 3D shape, providing a complete and accurate morphological characterization.
